guides-learn-context-management-014.text:
- messages : List of conversation messages (system, user, assistant, tool)
- tools : Optional ToolsSchema defining available functions
- tool_choice : Optional strategy for tool selection

guides-learn-context-management-015.text: Context can also include tools (function definitions) that the LLM can call during conversations:

guides-learn-context-management-016.text:
from pipecat.adapters.schemas.function_schema import FunctionSchema
from pipecat.adapters.schemas.tools_schema import ToolsSchema

# Define available functions
weather_function = FunctionSchema(
    name="get_current_weather",
    description="Get the current weather",
    properties={
        "location": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "The city and state, e.g. San Francisco, CA",
        },
        "format": {
            "type": "string",
            "enum": ["celsius", "fahrenheit"],
            "description": "The temperature unit to use.",
        },
    },
    required=["location", "format"],
)

# Create tools schema
tools = ToolsSchema(standard_tools=[weather_function])

# Create context with both messages and tools
context = LLMContext(messages, tools)
user_aggregator, assistant_aggregator = LLMContextAggregatorPair(context)

guides-learn-context-management-017.text: Function call results are also automatically stored in the context, maintaining a complete conversation history including tool interactions.

guides-learn-context-management-018.text:
We’ll cover function calling in detail in an upcoming section. The context
aggregator handles function call storage automatically.

guides-learn-context-management-019.h3: 3. Add Context Aggregators to Your Pipeline
guides-learn-context-management-019.text:
pipeline = Pipeline([
    transport.input(),
    stt,
    context_aggregator.user(),      # User context aggregator
    llm,
    tts,
    transport.output(),
    context_aggregator.assistant(), # Assistant context aggregator
])

guides-learn-context-management-020.h2: System Instruction vs Context System Messages
guides-learn-context-management-020.text: There are two ways to provide a system prompt to your LLM:

guides-learn-context-management-021.h2: System Instruction vs Context System Messages
guides-learn-context-management-021.text: Set the system prompt via the LLM service’s Settings. The service automatically prepends it to the context messages on each request:

guides-learn-context-management-022.h2: System Instruction vs Context System Messages
guides-learn-context-management-022.text:
llm = OpenAILLMService(
    api_key=os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY"),
    settings=OpenAILLMService.Settings(
        model="gpt-4o",
        system_instruction="You are a helpful voice assistant.",
    ),
)

# Context only needs conversation messages
context = LLMContext()

guides-learn-context-management-023.h2: System Instruction vs Context System Messages

guides-learn-context-management-024.h2: System Instruction vs Context System Messages
guides-learn-context-management-024.text:
- Survives context updates : The system prompt is always prepended, even after LLMMessagesUpdateFrame replaces the context or context summarization compresses older messages.
- Shared context : Multiple LLM services can share a single LLMContext while each provides its own system instruction.
- Runtime updates : You can change the system prompt mid-conversation via LLMUpdateSettingsFrame .

guides-learn-context-management-025.h2: System Instruction vs Context System Messages
guides-learn-context-management-025.text: You can also include a system message directly in the context messages:

guides-learn-context-management-026.h2: System Instruction vs Context System Messages
guides-learn-context-management-026.text:
messages = [
    {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful voice assistant."},
]
context = LLMContext(messages)

guides-learn-context-management-027.h2: System Instruction vs Context System Messages
guides-learn-context-management-027.text: This works but has limitations: the system message can be lost during context summarization or full context replacement, and it cannot be shared across multiple LLM services with different system prompts.

guides-learn-context-management-028.h2: System Instruction vs Context System Messages
guides-learn-context-management-028.text:
If both
system_instruction
and a system message in the context are set,
system_instruction
takes precedence and a warning is logged. Avoid using both at the same time.

guides-learn-context-management-029.text: The placement of context aggregator instances in your pipeline is crucial for proper operation:

guides-learn-context-management-030.text: Place the user context aggregator downstream from the STT service . Since the user’s speech results in TranscriptionFrame objects pushed by the STT service, the user aggregator needs to be positioned to collect these frames.

guides-learn-context-management-031.text: Place the assistant context aggregator after transport.output() . This positioning is important because:

guides-learn-context-management-032.text:
- The TTS service outputs TTSTextFrame s in addition to audio
- The assistant aggregator must be downstream to collect those frames
- It ensures context updates happen word-by-word for specific services (e.g. Cartesia, ElevenLabs, and Rime)
- Your context stays updated at the word level in case an interruption occurs

guides-learn-context-management-033.text:
**after**
Always place the assistant context aggregator

transport.output()
to ensure proper word-level context updates during interruptions.

guides-learn-context-management-034.text: You can programmatically add new messages to the context by pushing or queueing specific frames:

guides-learn-context-management-035.text:
- LLMMessagesAppendFrame : Appends a new message to the existing context
- LLMMessagesUpdateFrame : Completely replaces the existing context with new messages

guides-learn-context-management-037.text: The context aggregator provides a context property for getting the current context:

guides-learn-context-management-039.text:
In long-running conversations, context grows with every exchange, increasing token usage and potentially hitting context window limits. Pipecat includes built-in context summarization that automatically compresses older conversation history while preserving recent messages.

Enable it by setting enable_context_summarization=True when creating your context aggregators:

guides-learn-context-management-041.text:
Learn how to configure summarization triggers, customize behavior, and control
what gets preserved.

guides-learn-context-management-042.text: You may want to manually trigger the bot to speak in two scenarios:

guides-learn-context-management-043.text:
1. Starting a pipeline where the bot should speak first
2. After editing the context using LLMMessagesAppendFrame or LLMMessagesUpdateFrame

guides-learn-context-management-044.text:
# Example: Bot speaks first when pipeline starts
@transport.event_handler("on_client_connected")
async def on_client_connected(transport, client):
    # Trigger a response
    await task.queue_frames([LLMRunFrame()])

guides-learn-context-management-046.text: This gives you fine-grained control over when and how the bot responds during the conversation flow.

guides-learn-context-management-047.text:
- Context is conversation history - automatically maintained as users and bots exchange messages
- Use system_instruction for system prompts - set it in LLM Settings rather than as a context message for reliability across context updates and summarization
- Frame types matter - TranscriptionFrame for users, TTSTextFrame for assistants
- Placement matters - user aggregator after STT, assistant aggregator after transport output
- Tools are included - function definitions and results are stored in context
- Manual control available - use frames to append messages or trigger responses when needed
- Word-level precision - proper placement ensures context accuracy during interruptions
- Automatic summarization - enable context summarization to manage long conversations efficiently and reduce token costs

guides-learn-context-management-048.text: Now that you understand context management, let’s explore how to configure the LLM services that process this context to generate intelligent responses.

guides-learn-context-management-049.text: Learn how to configure language models in your voice AI pipeline

guides-learn-function-calling-000.text: Function calling (also known as tool calling) allows LLMs to request information from external services and APIs during conversations. This extends your voice AI bot’s capabilities beyond its training data to access real-time information and perform actions.

guides-learn-function-calling-001.text: Function calling works seamlessly within your existing pipeline structure. The LLM service handles function calls automatically when they’re needed:

guides-learn-function-calling-002.text:
pipeline = Pipeline([
    transport.input(),
    stt,
    context_aggregator.user(),     # Collects user transcriptions
    llm,                          # Processes context, calls functions when needed
    tts,
    transport.output(),
    context_aggregator.assistant(), # Collects function results and responses
])

guides-learn-function-calling-004.text:
1. User asks a question requiring external data
2. LLM recognizes the need and calls appropriate function
3. Your function handler executes and returns results
4. LLM incorporates results into its response
5. Response flows to TTS and user as normal

guides-learn-function-calling-005.text: Context integration: Function calls and their results are automatically stored in conversation context by the context aggregators, maintaining complete conversation history.

guides-learn-function-calling-006.text:
Function calling allows your bot to access real-time data and perform actions that aren’t part of its training data.

For example, you could give your bot the ability to:

guides-learn-function-calling-007.text:
- Check current weather conditions
- Look up stock prices
- Query a database
- Control smart home devices
- Schedule appointments

guides-learn-function-calling-009.text:
1. You define functions the LLM can use and register them to the LLM service used in your pipeline
2. When needed, the LLM requests a function call
3. Your application executes any corresponding functions
4. The result is sent back to the LLM
5. The LLM uses this information in its response

guides-learn-function-calling-010.text:
Pipecat provides a standardized FunctionSchema that works across all supported LLM providers. This makes it easy to define functions once and use them with any provider.

As a shorthand, you could also bypass specifying a function configuration at all and instead use “direct” functions. Under the hood, these are converted to FunctionSchema s.

**​Using the Standard Schema (Recommended)**

guides-learn-function-calling-011.text:
from pipecat.adapters.schemas.function_schema import FunctionSchema
from pipecat.adapters.schemas.tools_schema import ToolsSchema

# Define a function using the standard schema
weather_function = FunctionSchema(
    name="get_current_weather",
    description="Get the current weather in a location",
    properties={
        "location": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "The city and state, e.g. San Francisco, CA",
        },
        "format": {
            "type": "string",
            "enum": ["celsius", "fahrenheit"],
            "description": "The temperature unit to use.",
        },
    },
    required=["location", "format"]
)

# Create a tools schema with your functions
tools = ToolsSchema(standard_tools=[weather_function])

# Pass this to your LLM context
context = LLMContext(
    messages=[{"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."}],
    tools=tools
)
user_aggregator, assistant_aggregator = LLMContextAggregatorPair(context)

guides-learn-function-calling-012.text:
The ToolsSchema will be automatically converted to the correct format for your LLM provider through adapters.

**​Using Direct Functions (Shorthand)**

You can bypass specifying a function configuration (as a FunctionSchema or in a provider-specific format) and instead pass the function directly to your ToolsSchema . Pipecat will auto-configure the function, gathering relevant metadata from its signature and docstring. Metadata includes:

guides-learn-function-calling-013.text:
- name
- description
- properties (including individual property descriptions)
- list of required properties

guides-learn-function-calling-014.text: Note that the function signature is a bit different when using direct functions. The first parameter is FunctionCallParams , followed by any others necessary for the function.

guides-learn-function-calling-015.text:
from pipecat.adapters.schemas.tools_schema import ToolsSchema
from pipecat.services.llm_service import FunctionCallParams

# Define a direct function
async def get_current_weather(params: FunctionCallParams, location: str, format: str):
    """Get the current weather.

    Args:
        location: The city and state, e.g. "San Francisco, CA".
        format: The temperature unit to use. Must be either "celsius" or "fahrenheit".
    """
    weather_data = {"conditions": "sunny", "temperature": "75"}
    await params.result_callback(weather_data)

# Create a tools schema, passing your function directly to it
tools = ToolsSchema(standard_tools=[get_current_weather])

# Pass this to your LLM context
context = LLMContext(
    messages=[{"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."}],
    tools=tools
)
user_aggregator, assistant_aggregator = LLMContextAggregatorPair(context)

guides-learn-function-calling-016.text:
**​Using Provider-Specific Formats (Alternative)**

You can also define functions in the provider-specific format if needed:

guides-learn-function-calling-018.text:
**​Provider-Specific Custom Tools**

Some providers support unique tools that don’t fit the standard function schema. For these cases, you can add custom tools:

guides-learn-function-calling-020.text:
See the provider-specific documentation for details on custom tools and their
formats.

guides-learn-function-calling-021.text: Register handlers for your functions using one of these LLM service methods :

guides-learn-function-calling-023.text: Which one you use depends on whether your function is a “direct” function .

guides-learn-function-calling-024.text:
from pipecat.services.llm_service import FunctionCallParams

llm = OpenAILLMService(api_key="your-api-key")

# Main function handler - called to execute the function
async def fetch_weather_from_api(params: FunctionCallParams):
    # Fetch weather data from your API
    weather_data = {"conditions": "sunny", "temperature": "75"}
    await params.result_callback(weather_data)

# Register the function
llm.register_function(
    "get_current_weather",
    fetch_weather_from_api,
    cancel_on_interruption=True,  # Cancel if user interrupts (default: True)
    timeout_secs=30.0,  # Optional: Override global timeout for this function
)

guides-learn-function-calling-026.text:
- cancel_on_interruption=True (default): Function call is cancelled if user interrupts
- cancel_on_interruption=False : Function call continues even if user interrupts
- timeout_secs=None (default): Optional per-tool timeout in seconds. Overrides the global function_call_timeout_secs for this specific function

guides-learn-function-calling-027.text:
Use cancel_on_interruption=False for critical operations that should complete even if the user starts speaking. Function calls are async, so you can continue the conversation while the function executes. Once the result returns, the LLM will automatically incorporate it into the conversation context. LLMs vary in terms of how well they incorporate changes to previous messages, so you may need to experiment with your LLM provider to see how it handles this.

Use timeout_secs to set a specific timeout for a function that differs from the global default. For example, you might want a longer timeout for database queries or shorter timeouts for quick lookups.

guides-learn-function-calling-028.text: Include your LLM service in your pipeline with the registered functions:

guides-learn-function-calling-029.text:
# Initialize the LLM context with your function schemas
context = LLMContext(
    messages=[{"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."}],
    tools=tools
)

# Create the context aggregator to collect the user and assistant context
user_aggregator, assistant_aggregator = LLMContextAggregatorPair(context)

# Create the pipeline
pipeline = Pipeline([
    transport.input(),     # Input from the transport
    stt,                   # STT processing
    user_aggregator,       # User context aggregation
    llm,                   # LLM processing
    tts,                   # TTS processing
    transport.output(),    # Output to the transport
    assistant_aggregator,  # Assistant context aggregation
])

guides-learn-function-calling-030.text: Every function handler receives a FunctionCallParams object containing all the information needed for execution:

guides-learn-function-calling-031.text:
@dataclass
class FunctionCallParams:
    function_name: str                          # Name of the called function
    tool_call_id: str                           # Unique identifier for this call
    arguments: Mapping[str, Any]                # Arguments from the LLM
    llm: LLMService                             # Reference to the LLM service
    context: LLMContext                         # Current conversation context
    result_callback: FunctionCallResultCallback # Return results here

guides-learn-function-calling-033.text:
async def example_function_handler(params: FunctionCallParams):
    # Access function details
    print(f"Called function: {params.function_name}")
    print(f"Call ID: {params.tool_call_id}")

    # Extract arguments
    location = params.arguments["location"]

    # Access LLM context for conversation history
    messages = params.context.messages

    # Use LLM service for additional operations
    await params.llm.push_frame(TTSSpeakFrame("Looking up weather data..."))

    # Return results
    await params.result_callback({"conditions": "nice", "temperature": "75"})

guides-learn-function-calling-034.text: See the API reference for complete details.

guides-learn-function-calling-036.text:
1. Receive necessary arguments, either:
  - From params.arguments
  - Directly from function arguments, if using direct functions
2. Process data or call external services
3. Return results via params.result_callback(result)

guides-learn-function-calling-038.text: When returning results from a function handler, you can control how the LLM processes those results using a FunctionCallResultProperties object passed to the result callback.

guides-learn-function-calling-039.text: FunctionCallResultProperties provides fine-grained control over LLM execution:

guides-learn-function-calling-042.text:
- run_llm=True : Run LLM after function call (default behavior)
- run_llm=False : Don’t run LLM after function call (useful for chained calls)
- on_context_updated : Async callback executed after the function result is added to context

guides-learn-function-calling-043.text:
Skip LLM execution (
run_llm=False
) when you have back-to-back function
calls. If you skip a completion, you must manually trigger one from the
context aggregator.

guides-learn-function-calling-044.text: See the API reference for complete details.

guides-learn-function-calling-045.text:
from pipecat.frames.frames import FunctionCallResultProperties
from pipecat.services.llm_service import FunctionCallParams

async def fetch_weather_from_api(params: FunctionCallParams):
    # Fetch weather data
    weather_data = {"conditions": "sunny", "temperature": "75"}

    # Don't run LLM after this function call
    properties = FunctionCallResultProperties(run_llm=False)

    await params.result_callback(weather_data, properties=properties)

async def query_database(params: FunctionCallParams):
    # Query database
    results = await db.query(params.arguments["query"])

    async def on_update():
        await notify_system("Database query complete")

    # Run LLM after function call and notify when context is updated
    properties = FunctionCallResultProperties(
        run_llm=True,
        on_context_updated=on_update
    )

    await params.result_callback(results, properties=properties)

guides-learn-function-calling-046.text:
- Function calling extends LLM capabilities beyond training data to real-time information
- Context integration is automatic - function calls and results are stored in conversation history
- Multiple definition approaches - use standard schema for portability, direct functions for simplicity
- Pipeline integration is seamless - functions work within your existing voice AI architecture
- Advanced control available - fine-tune LLM execution and monitor function call lifecycle

guides-learn-function-calling-047.text: Now that you understand function calling, let’s explore how to configure text-to-speech services to convert your LLM’s responses (including function call results) into natural-sounding speech.

guides-learn-function-calling-048.text: Learn how to configure speech synthesis in your voice AI pipeline

guides-learn-llm-000.text: LLM services are responsible for chat completions and tool calling based on the provided context (conversation history). The LLM responds by streaming tokens via LLMTextFrame s, which are used by subsequent processors to create audio output for the bot.

guides-learn-llm-001.text: The LLM instance should be placed after the user context aggregator and before any downstream services that depend on the LLM’s output stream:

guides-learn-llm-002.text:
pipeline = Pipeline([
    transport.input(),
    stt,                           # Creates TranscriptionFrames
    context_aggregator.user(),     # Processes user context → creates LLMContextFrame
    llm,                           # Processes context → streams LLMTextFrames
    tts,                           # Processes LLMTextFrames → creates TTSAudioRawFrames
    transport.output(),
    context_aggregator.assistant(),
])

guides-learn-llm-004.text:
- Input : Receives LLMContextFrame containing conversation history
- Processing :
  - Analyzes context and generates streaming response
  - Handles function calls if tools are available
  - Tracks token usage for metrics
- Output :
  - Denotes the start of the streaming response by pushing an LLMFullResponseStartFrame
  - Streams LLMTextFrame s containing response tokens to downstream processors (enables real-time TTS processing)
  - Ends with an LLMFullResponseEndFrame to mark the completion of the response
  - Output frames can be configured to skip TTS via LLMConfigureOutputFrame(skip_tts=True) , allowing text to flow through the pipeline without being spoken
- Function calls:
  - FunctionCallsStartedFrame : Indicates function execution beginning
  - FunctionCallInProgressFrame : Indicates a function is currently executing
  - FunctionCallResultFrame : Contains results from executed functions

guides-learn-llm-005.text: Pipecat supports a wide range of LLM providers to fit different needs, performance requirements, and budgets:

guides-learn-llm-006.text: Most LLM services are built on the OpenAI chat completion specification for compatibility:

guides-learn-llm-007.text: GPT models with the original chat completion API

guides-learn-llm-008.text: Claude models with advanced reasoning capabilities

guides-learn-llm-010.text:
Enterprise-grade hosting for various foundation models

Compatible APIs : Any OpenAI-spec compatible service can be used via the base_url parameter.

guides-learn-llm-011.text: For lower latency, some providers offer direct speech-to-speech models:

guides-learn-llm-012.text:
- OpenAI Realtime : Direct speech input/output with GPT models
- Gemini Live : Real-time speech conversations with Gemini
- AWS Nova Sonic : Speech-optimized models on Bedrock

guides-learn-llm-013.text: For a complete list of supported LLM services, see the Supported Services page:

guides-learn-llm-014.text: View the complete list of supported language model providers

guides-learn-llm-015.text: Many LLM services use the OpenAI chat completion specification. Pipecat provides a BaseOpenAILLMService that most providers extend, enabling easy switching between compatible services:

guides-learn-llm-016.text:
from pipecat.services.openai.llm import OpenAILLMService

# Native OpenAI
llm = OpenAILLMService(api_key=os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY"))

# OpenAI-compatible service via base_url
llm = OpenAILLMService(
    api_key=os.getenv("OTHER_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://api.other-provider.com/v1"  # Custom endpoint
)

guides-learn-llm-017.text: This architecture allows you to quickly plug in different LLM services without changing your pipeline code.

guides-learn-llm-018.text: Each LLM service has its own configuration options. For example, configuring OpenAI with various parameters:

guides-learn-llm-019.text:
from pipecat.services.openai.llm import OpenAILLMService

llm = OpenAILLMService(
    api_key=os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY"),
    settings=OpenAILLMService.Settings(
        model="gpt-4.1",
        system_instruction="You are a helpful voice assistant.",
        temperature=0.7,            # Response creativity (0.0-2.0)
        max_completion_tokens=150,  # Maximum response length
        frequency_penalty=0.5,      # Reduce repetition (0.0-2.0)
        presence_penalty=0.5,       # Encourage topic diversity (0.0-2.0)
    ),
)

guides-learn-llm-020.text: For detailed configuration options specific to each provider:

guides-learn-llm-021.text: Explore configuration options for each supported LLM provider

guides-learn-llm-022.text: All LLM services inherit from the LLMService base class with shared configuration options:

guides-learn-llm-023.text:
llm = YourLLMService(
    # Service-specific options...
    run_in_parallel=True,  # Whether function calls run in parallel (default: True)
)

guides-learn-llm-025.text:
- run_in_parallel : Controls whether function calls execute simultaneously or sequentially
  - True (default): Faster execution when multiple functions are called
  - False : Sequential execution for dependent function calls

guides-learn-llm-026.text: LLM services provide event handlers for monitoring completion lifecycle:

guides-learn-llm-027.text:
@llm.event_handler("on_completion_timeout")
async def on_completion_timeout(service):
    logger.warning("LLM completion timed out")
    # Handle timeout (retry, fallback, etc.)

@llm.event_handler("on_function_calls_started")
async def on_function_calls_started(service, function_calls):
    logger.info(f"Starting {len(function_calls)} function calls")
    # Optionally notify user that bot is "thinking"
    await tts.queue_frame(TTSSpeakFrame("Let me check on that."))

guides-learn-llm-029.text:
- on_completion_timeout : Triggered when LLM requests timeout
- on_function_calls_started : Triggered when function calls are initiated

guides-learn-llm-030.text: These handlers enable you to provide user feedback and implement error recovery strategies.

guides-learn-llm-031.text:
LLMs can call external functions to access real-time data and perform actions beyond their training data. This enables capabilities like checking weather, querying databases, or controlling external APIs.

Function calls and their results are automatically stored in the conversation context by the context aggregator.

guides-learn-llm-032.text: Learn how to enable LLMs to interact with external services and APIs

guides-learn-llm-033.text:
- Pipeline placement matters - LLM goes after user context, before TTS
- Token streaming enables real-time responses - no waiting for complete generation
- OpenAI compatibility enables easy provider switching
- Function calling extends capabilities beyond training data
- Configuration affects behavior - tune temperature, penalties, and limits
- Services are modular - swap providers without changing pipeline code

guides-learn-llm-034.text: Now that you understand LLM configuration, let’s explore how function calling enables your bot to interact with external services and real-time data.

guides-learn-llm-035.text: Learn how to enable LLMs to interact with external services and APIs

guides-learn-overview-000.text: This comprehensive guide will teach you how to build real-time voice AI agents with Pipecat. By the end, you’ll be equipped with the knowledge to create custom applications—from simple voice assistants to complex multimodal bots that can see, hear, and speak.

guides-learn-overview-001.text:
**Prerequisites**
: Basic Python knowledge is recommended. The guide takes
approximately 45-60 minutes to complete, with hands-on examples throughout.

guides-learn-overview-002.text: Building responsive voice AI applications involves coordinating multiple AI services in real-time:

guides-learn-overview-003.text:
- Speech recognition must transcribe audio as users speak
- Language models need to process context and generate responses
- Speech synthesis has to convert text back to natural audio
- Network transports must handle streaming audio with minimal delay

guides-learn-overview-004.text: Doing this manually means managing complex timing, buffering, error handling, and service coordination. Most developers end up rebuilding the same orchestration logic repeatedly.

guides-learn-overview-005.text:
Pipecat solves this orchestration problem with a pipeline architecture that handles the complexity for you. Instead of managing individual API calls and timing, you define a flow of processing steps that work together automatically.

Here’s what makes Pipecat different:

guides-learn-overview-006.text: Typical voice interactions complete in 500-800ms for natural conversations

guides-learn-overview-007.text:
Swap AI providers, add features, or customize behavior without rewriting
code

guides-learn-overview-008.text: Stream processing eliminates waiting for complete responses at each step

guides-learn-overview-009.text: Built-in error handling, logging, and scaling considerations

guides-learn-overview-010.text: Before diving into how voice AI works, let’s understand Pipecat’s three foundational concepts:

guides-learn-overview-011.text: Think of frames as data packages moving through your application. Each frame contains a specific type of information:

guides-learn-overview-012.text:
- Audio data from a microphone
- Transcribed text from speech recognition
- Generated responses from an LLM
- Synthesized audio for playback

guides-learn-overview-013.text: Frame processors are specialized workers that handle specific tasks:

guides-learn-overview-014.text:
- A speech-to-text processor converts audio frames into text frames
- An LLM processor takes text frames and produces response frames
- A text-to-speech processor converts response frames into audio frames

guides-learn-overview-015.text: Pipelines connect processors together , creating a path for frames to flow through your application. They handle the orchestration automatically.

guides-learn-overview-016.text: Now let’s see how these concepts work together in a typical voice AI interaction:

guides-learn-overview-017.text:
1
Audio Input
User speaks → Transport receives streaming audio → Creates audio frames
2
Speech Recognition
STT processor receives audio frames → Transcribes speech in real-time →
Outputs text frames
3
Context Management
Context processor aggregates text frames with conversation history → Creates
formatted input for LLM
4
Language Processing
LLM processor receives context → Generates streaming response → Outputs text
frames
5
Speech Synthesis
TTS processor receives text frames → Converts to speech → Outputs audio
frames
6
Audio Output
Transport receives audio frames → Streams to user’s device → User hears
response

guides-learn-overview-018.text: The key insight: everything happens in parallel . While the LLM is generating later parts of a response, earlier parts are already being converted to speech and played back to the user.

guides-learn-overview-019.text:
Here’s how this flow translates into a Pipecat pipeline:

Each processor in the pipeline:

guides-learn-overview-020.text:
1. Receives specific frame types as input
2. Performs its specialized task (transcription, language processing, etc.)
3. Outputs new frames for the next processor
4. Passes through frames it doesn’t handle

guides-learn-overview-021.text:
While frames can flow upstream or downstream, most data flows downstream as
shown above. We’ll discuss pushing frames in later sections.

guides-learn-overview-022.text: In the following sections, we’ll explore each component of this pipeline in detail:

guides-learn-overview-023.text:
- How to initialize sessions and connect users
- Configuring different transport options (Daily, WebRTC, Twilio, etc.)
- Setting up speech recognition and synthesis services
- Managing conversation context and LLM integration
- Handling the complete pipeline lifecycle
- Building custom processors for your specific needs

guides-learn-overview-024.text: Each section includes practical examples and configuration options to help you build production-ready voice AI applications.

guides-learn-overview-025.text:
Let’s begin with session initialization to connect users to your voice AI
agent

guides-learn-pipeline-000.text: The Pipeline is the core orchestration component in Pipecat that connects frame processors together, creating a structured path for data to flow through your voice AI application.

guides-learn-pipeline-001.text: A Pipeline takes a list of frame processors and connects them in sequence. Here’s a simple voice AI pipeline that matches the voice AI agent architecture we discussed earlier:

guides-learn-pipeline-002.text:
pipeline = Pipeline([
    transport.input(),              # Receives user audio
    stt,                            # Speech-to-text conversion
    context_aggregator.user(),      # Collect user responses
    llm,                            # Language model processing
    tts,                            # Text-to-speech conversion
    transport.output(),             # Sends audio to user
    context_aggregator.assistant(), # Collect assistant responses
])

guides-learn-pipeline-003.text: Before diving deeper into pipelines, it’s important to understand how data moves through them using frames and frame processors .

guides-learn-pipeline-004.text:
Frames are data containers that carry information through your pipeline. Think of them as packages on a conveyor belt. Each frame contains a specific type of data that processors can examine and act upon.

Frames automatically receive unique identifiers and names (like TranscriptionFrame#1 ) that help with debugging and tracking data flow through your pipeline.

Common frame types include:

guides-learn-pipeline-005.text:
- Audio frames : Raw audio data from users or generated by TTS
- Text frames : Transcriptions, LLM responses, or other text content
- Image frames : Visual data for multimodal applications
- System frames : Control signals for pipeline management
- Context frames : Conversation history and state information

guides-learn-pipeline-006.text: Frames flow through your pipeline from processor to processor, carrying the data your voice AI application needs to operate.

guides-learn-pipeline-007.text: Frame Processors are the workers in your pipeline. Each processor has a specific job - like converting speech to text, generating responses, or playing audio. They:

guides-learn-pipeline-008.text:
- Receive frames from the previous processor in the pipeline
- Process the data (transcribe audio, generate text, etc.)
- Create new frames with their output
- Pass frames along to the next processor

guides-learn-pipeline-009.text: Frame processors are modular and reusable. You can swap out different STT services or LLM providers without changing the rest of your pipeline.

guides-learn-pipeline-010.text: Frames in Pipecat have different base classes that determine how they’re processed:

guides-learn-pipeline-013.text:
- SystemFrames : Processed immediately (interruptions, pipeline control, user input)
- DataFrames & ControlFrames : Queued and processed in order (audio output, text, images)

guides-learn-pipeline-015.text:
# SystemFrames (processed immediately)
InputAudioRawFrame         # User audio input
UserStartedSpeakingFrame   # Speech detection events
StartInterruptionFrame     # Interruption control
ErrorFrame                 # Error notifications

# DataFrames (queued and ordered)
OutputAudioRawFrame        # Audio for playback
TextFrame                  # Text content
TranscriptionFrame         # Speech-to-text results
LLMTextFrame               # LLM responses
AggregatedTextFrame        # Text aggregated into a describable unit
TTSTextFrame               # Text-to-speech text output

# ControlFrames (queued and ordered)
EndFrame                   # Pipeline shutdown
TTSStartedFrame            # TTS response boundaries
LLMFullResponseStartFrame  # LLM response boundaries

guides-learn-pipeline-016.text: Frames are processed in guaranteed order , even across ParallelPipelines. This enables reliable sequencing. For example, you can push two frames in order and the order will be respected. Additionally, the corresponding processing will finish before allowing the next frame to be processed. Let’s look at an example where we push two frames— TTSSpeakFrame and EndFrame —in order to say goodbye then end the pipeline:

guides-learn-pipeline-017.text:
from pipecat.frames.frames import EndFrame, TTSSpeakFrame

# These will execute in order: speak first, then end pipeline
await task.queue_frames([
    TTSSpeakFrame("Goodbye!"),
    EndFrame()
])

guides-learn-pipeline-018.text: Every frame processor follows the same pattern with two key methods:

guides-learn-pipeline-019.text:
class TranscriptionLogger(FrameProcessor):
    async def process_frame(self, frame: Frame, direction: FrameDirection):
        # Always call parent first
        await super().process_frame(frame, direction)

        # Handle specific frame types
        if isinstance(frame, TranscriptionFrame):
            print(f"Transcription: {frame.text}")

        # Push frame to next processor
        await self.push_frame(frame, direction)

guides-learn-pipeline-021.text:
- process_frame() : Inspect and handle incoming frames
- push_frame() : Send frames upstream or downstream

guides-learn-pipeline-022.text: Learn how to build your own frame processors

guides-learn-pipeline-023.text: Understanding data flow is crucial for building effective pipelines:

guides-learn-pipeline-024.text: Order matters : Processors must be arranged so that each receives the frame types it needs:

guides-learn-pipeline-025.text:
1. transport.input() creates InputAudioRawFrame s from user audio
2. stt receives audio frames and outputs TranscriptionFrame s
3. llm processes text and generates LLMTextFrame s
4. tts converts text frames to TTSAudioRawFrame s, AggregatedTextFrame s, and TTSTextFrame s
5. transport.output() creates OutputAudioRawFrame s and sends audio back to user

guides-learn-pipeline-026.text:
- Note: An LLMTextProcessor can sit between the llm and tts to pre-aggregate LLMTextFrame s into AggregatedTextFrame s. This simply moves the aggregation step
out of the TTS.

guides-learn-pipeline-027.text: Processors always push frames : Processors don’t consume frames, they pass them along:

guides-learn-pipeline-028.text:
# This pipeline allows multiple processors to use the same audio
pipeline = Pipeline([
    transport.input(),          # Creates InputAudioRawFrame
    stt,                        # Uses audio → creates TranscriptionFrame
    # ...                       # Other processors can use the same audio
    tts,                        # Uses various text frames → creates TTSAudioRawFrame
    transport.output(),         # Uses TTSAudioRawFrame → sends audio to user
    audio_buffer_processor,     # Also uses the same user and assistant audio for recording
])

guides-learn-pipeline-029.text: This design allows multiple processors to operate on the same data stream without interfering with each other.

guides-learn-pipeline-030.text: Use ParallelPipeline to create branches where each branch receives all upstream frames. Frames are collected and pushed individually from each branch:

guides-learn-pipeline-031.text:
pipeline = Pipeline([
    transport.input(),
    stt,
    context_aggregator.user(),
    llm,
    ParallelPipeline([
        # English branch
        [FunctionFilter(english_filter), english_tts],
        # Spanish branch
        [FunctionFilter(spanish_filter), spanish_tts],
    ]),
    transport.output(),
    context_aggregator.assistant(),
])

guides-learn-pipeline-033.text:
- Both TTS branches receive all LLM output
- Each branch can filter and process frames independently
- Results from both branches flow to transport.output()

guides-learn-pipeline-034.text: ParallelPipelines are traditionally paired with filters or gates to control which frames go where, allowing for complex conditional logic.

guides-learn-pipeline-035.text: Frame processors have internal queues that ensure ordered processing:

guides-learn-pipeline-036.text:
- SystemFrames bypass queues for immediate processing (interruptions, errors)
- DataFrames and ControlFrames are queued and processed in order
- Queuing is managed automatically by the pipeline infrastructure
- Order is guaranteed even across complex pipeline structures

guides-learn-pipeline-037.text:
Learn more about frame flow patterns in the
Custom Frame Processor
Guide
.

guides-learn-pipeline-038.text: Wrap your pipeline in a PipelineTask to configure execution parameters:

guides-learn-pipeline-039.text:
task = PipelineTask(
    pipeline,
    params=PipelineParams(
        enable_metrics=True,
        enable_usage_metrics=True,
        audio_in_sample_rate=8000,
        audio_out_sample_rate=8000,
    ),
    observers=[RTVIObserver(rtvi)],
)

guides-learn-pipeline-040.text: Use PipelineRunner to execute your pipeline task:

guides-learn-pipeline-041.text:
# Create the runner
runner = PipelineRunner(handle_sigint=False)

# Run the pipeline
await runner.run(task)

guides-learn-pipeline-043.text:
- Lifecycle Management : Handles startup, execution, and cleanup
- Signal Handling : Responds to SIGINT/SIGTERM for graceful shutdown (when handle_sigint=True )
- Resource Cleanup : Ensures proper disposal of resources when tasks complete

guides-learn-pipeline-045.text:
# Handle system signals automatically
# Useful when running a file directly
runner = PipelineRunner(handle_sigint=True)

# Custom signal handling
# Useful when the PipelineRunner is managed by
# another system like the development runner
runner = PipelineRunner(handle_sigint=False)

guides-learn-pipeline-046.text: The pipeline runs continuously, processing frames as they arrive, until one of these conditions occurs:

guides-learn-pipeline-047.text:
1. Normal Completion : Bot code returns (session ends)
2. Signal Termination : SIGINT or SIGTERM received (if handle_sigint=True )
3. Task Cancellation : Explicit call to task.cancel()
4. Error Condition : Unhandled exception in pipeline

guides-learn-pipeline-049.text:
# Graceful shutdown on client disconnect
@transport.event_handler("on_client_disconnected")
async def on_client_disconnected(transport, client):
    logger.info("Client disconnected - stopping pipeline")
    await task.cancel()

# Await the pipeline task
await runner.run(task)

guides-learn-pipeline-051.text:
- Starting : Initializing processors and connections
- Running : Actively processing frames and handling events
- Stopping : Gracefully shutting down processors
- Stopped : All resources cleaned up

guides-learn-pipeline-052.text: Beyond the basic pipeline structure, you can fine-tune behavior through PipelineParams and add monitoring capabilities:

guides-learn-pipeline-053.text: PipelineParams lets you configure how your entire pipeline operates:

guides-learn-pipeline-054.text:
params = PipelineParams(
    # Audio configuration (affects all audio processors)
    audio_in_sample_rate=16000,
    audio_out_sample_rate=24000,

    # Performance monitoring
    enable_metrics=True,
    enable_usage_metrics=True,

    # Debugging and development
    send_initial_empty_metrics=False,
    report_only_initial_ttfb=True,
)

guides-learn-pipeline-055.text: Add observers to monitor pipeline events and track custom application metrics:

guides-learn-pipeline-056.text:
task = PipelineTask(
    pipeline,
    params=params,
    observers=[
        RTVIObserver(rtvi),           # RTVI protocol events
        CustomMetricsObserver(),      # Your custom metrics
    ],
)

guides-learn-pipeline-057.text: These configuration options enable you to optimize performance, debug issues, and monitor your pipeline in production:

guides-learn-pipeline-058.text: Configure execution parameters, audio settings, and debugging options

guides-learn-pipeline-059.text: Monitor pipeline performance, latency, and API usage in real-time

guides-learn-pipeline-060.text: Monitor pipeline events, frame processing, and custom application events

guides-learn-pipeline-061.text:
- Order matters - arrange processors so each gets the frames it needs
- Processors push frames - processors pass frames downstream, not consume them
- Frame types determine processing - SystemFrames bypass queues, others are ordered
- Queuing ensures reliability - frames are processed in guaranteed order
- Parallel processing enables conditional logic and multi-modal handling
- PipelineTask configures execution parameters and monitoring
- PipelineRunner manages the complete pipeline lifecycle
- PipelineParams control pipeline-wide behavior like audio settings and metrics

guides-learn-pipeline-062.text: Now that you understand how pipelines orchestrate processing, let’s explore the different transport options that connect your pipeline to users.

guides-learn-pipeline-063.text: Learn about the different ways users can connect to your voice AI pipeline

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-000.text: Pipeline termination ensures your voice AI applications shut down cleanly without resource leaks or hanging processes. Understanding the different termination methods helps you handle various scenarios from natural conversation endings to unexpected disconnections.

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-001.text: Pipeline termination works through the same frame-based system as other pipeline operations:

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-002.text:
pipeline = Pipeline([
    transport.input(),
    stt,
    context_aggregator.user(),
    llm,
    tts,
    transport.output(),
    context_aggregator.assistant(),
])
# EndFrame or CancelFrame flows through entire pipeline for shutdown

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-004.text:
- EndFrame : A queued ControlFrame that triggers graceful shutdown after processing pending frames
- CancelFrame : A SystemFrame that triggers immediate shutdown, discarding pending frames

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-005.text: Both frames flow downstream through the pipeline, allowing each processor to clean up resources appropriately.

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-006.text: Pipecat provides two primary approaches for pipeline termination, each designed for different scenarios:

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-007.text:
Graceful termination allows the bot to complete its current processing before shutting down. This is ideal when you want the bot to properly end a conversation. For example, after completing a specific task or reaching a natural conclusion.

When to use:

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-008.text:
- Natural conversation endings
- Task completion scenarios
- When the bot should say goodbye

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-009.text:
Implementation options:

Push an EndFrame from outside your pipeline:

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-010.text:
# From outside the pipeline
from pipecat.frames.frames import EndFrame, TTSSpeakFrame

await task.queue_frame(EndFrame())

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-011.text: Push an EndTaskFrame upstream from inside your pipeline:

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-012.text:
# From inside a function call
from pipecat.frames.frames import EndTaskFrame, TTSSpeakFrame
from pipecat.processors.frame_processor import FrameDirection

async def end_conversation(params: FunctionCallParams):
    await params.llm.push_frame(TTSSpeakFrame("Have a nice day!"))

    # Signal that the task should end after processing this frame
    await params.llm.push_frame(EndTaskFrame(), FrameDirection.UPSTREAM)

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-014.text:
1. EndFrame is queued and processes after any pending frames (like goodbye messages)
2. All processors shutdown when they receive the EndFrame
3. Once the EndFrame reaches the end of the pipeline, shutdown is complete
4. Resources are cleaned up and the process terminates

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-015.text:
Graceful termination allows your bot to say goodbye and complete any final
actions before terminating.

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-016.text:
Immediate termination cancels the pipeline without waiting for pending frames to complete. This is appropriate when the user is no longer active in the conversation.

When to use:

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-017.text:
- User disconnections (browser closed, call ended)
- Error conditions requiring immediate shutdown
- When completing the conversation is no longer necessary

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-018.text:
Implementation:

Use event handlers to detect disconnections and trigger cancellation:

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-019.text:
@transport.event_handler("on_client_disconnected")
async def on_client_disconnected(transport, client):
    logger.info("Client disconnected - terminating pipeline")
    await task.cancel()

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-021.text:
1. An event triggers the cancellation (like client disconnection)
2. task.cancel() pushes a CancelFrame downstream from the PipelineTask
3. CancelFrame s are SystemFrame s and bypass queues for immediate processing
4. Processors handle the CancelFrame and shut down immediately
5. Any pending frames are discarded during shutdown

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-022.text:
Immediate termination will discard any pending frames in the pipeline. Use
this approach when completing the conversation is no longer necessary.

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-023.text:
Pipecat includes automatic idle detection to prevent hanging pipelines. This feature monitors activity and can automatically cancel tasks when no meaningful bot interactions occur for an extended period.

How it works:

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-024.text:
- Monitors pipeline activity for meaningful bot interactions
- Automatically triggers termination after configured idle timeout
- Serves as a safety net for anomalous behavior or forgotten sessions

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-026.text:
task = PipelineTask(
    pipeline,
    # Configure idle detection timeout
    cancel_on_idle_timeout=True, # Default is True
    idle_timeout_seconds=600,  # Default is 300 seconds
    idle_timeout_frames=(BotSpeakingFrame,), # Default is (BotSpeakingFrame, LLMFullResponseEndFrame)
)

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-027.text: You can further configure the idle detection behavior. To learn more, refer to the Pipeline Idle Detection documentation:

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-028.text: Learn how to configure and customize idle detection for your use case

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-029.text:
Pipeline Idle Detection is enabled by default and helps prevent resources from
being wasted on inactive conversations.

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-030.text: Connect termination to transport events for automatic cleanup:

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-031.text:
@transport.event_handler("on_client_connected")
async def on_client_connected(transport, client):
    logger.info("Client connected - starting conversation")
    await task.queue_frames([LLMRunFrame()])

@transport.event_handler("on_client_disconnected")
async def on_client_disconnected(transport, client):
    logger.info("Client disconnected - immediate termination")
    await task.cancel()

# Run the pipeline
runner = PipelineRunner(handle_sigint=False)
await runner.run(task)

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-032.text: Use function calling or other logic to determine when conversations should end:

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-033.text:
async def check_conversation_complete(params: FunctionCallParams):
    # Your logic to determine if conversation should end
    conversation_complete = await evaluate_completion_criteria()

    if conversation_complete:
        await params.llm.push_frame(TTSSpeakFrame("Thank you for using our service!"))
        await params.llm.push_frame(EndTaskFrame(), FrameDirection.UPSTREAM)

    await params.result_callback({"status": "complete" if conversation_complete else "continuing"})

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-034.text: Ensure pipelines can terminate properly even when exceptions occur:

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-035.text:
try:
    runner = PipelineRunner(handle_sigint=False)
    await runner.run(task)
except Exception as e:
    logger.error(f"Pipeline error: {e}")
    # Ensure cleanup happens even on errors
    await task.cancel()

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-036.text: If your pipeline isn’t shutting down properly, check these common issues:

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-037.text:
Problem: Custom processors that don’t call push_frame() can block termination frames from reaching the end of the pipeline.

Solution: Ensure your custom processors propagate all frames downstream, including EndFrame and CancelFrame :

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-038.text:
async def process_frame(self, frame: Frame, direction: FrameDirection):
    await super().process_frame(frame, direction)

    # Your custom processing logic here

    # Always push frames downstream (including termination frames)
    await self.push_frame(frame, direction)

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-039.text:
Problem: Pushing EndFrame or CancelFrame from the middle of the pipeline may not reach the pipeline source properly.

Solution: Use the appropriate frame type and direction:

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-040.text:
# From inside the pipeline, push upstream to reach the source
await self.push_frame(EndTaskFrame(), FrameDirection.UPSTREAM)
await self.push_frame(CancelTaskFrame(), FrameDirection.UPSTREAM)

# The pipeline source will then convert these to proper termination frames
# and push them downstream through the entire pipeline

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-041.text:
The pipeline source automatically converts
EndTaskFrame
to
EndFrame
and
CancelTaskFrame
to
CancelFrame
when pushing downstream, ensuring proper
termination handling throughout the pipeline.

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-042.text:
- Frame-based termination - shutdown uses the same frame system as processing
- Choose the right method - graceful for natural endings, immediate for disconnections
- Event handlers enable automatic termination - respond to user disconnections cleanly
- Idle detection provides safety net - prevents hanging processes and resource waste
- SystemFrames bypass queues - CancelFrames process immediately for fast shutdown
- Resource cleanup is automatic - proper termination ensures clean resource disposal

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-043.text: You now understand how to build, run, and properly terminate voice AI pipelines! Next, let’s explore advanced development tools that can accelerate your Pipecat development process.

guides-learn-pipeline-termination-044.text: You’ve completed the learning guide! Continue to see recommended next steps.

guides-learn-session-initialization-000.text: Before your voice AI bot can start processing audio and generating responses, you need to establish a connection between the user and your bot. This process is called session initialization - it’s how users and bots find each other and establish a communication channel for real-time audio exchange.

guides-learn-session-initialization-001.text: Session initialization involves multiple components working together:

guides-learn-session-initialization-002.text:
- Runner : A FastAPI server that handles incoming connection requests and manages session setup
- Pipecat Bot : Your voice AI application running as a separate server-side service
- Client Application : The user-facing app (web browser, mobile app, etc.)

guides-learn-session-initialization-003.text: The runner acts as the coordinator, setting up the necessary resources and starting bot instances, while the Pipecat bot handles the actual voice AI processing.

guides-learn-session-initialization-004.text: For most development and many production use cases, Pipecat provides a development runner that handles all the session initialization complexity for you. Instead of building FastAPI servers and managing WebRTC connections yourself, you focus on your bot logic while the runner handles the infrastructure.

guides-learn-session-initialization-005.text: Your bot needs a single entry point function that the runner will call:

guides-learn-session-initialization-006.text:
from pipecat.runner.types import RunnerArguments

async def bot(runner_args: RunnerArguments):
    """Main bot entry point called by the development runner."""

    # Create your transport based on the runner arguments
    transport = SmallWebRTCTransport(
        params=TransportParams(
            audio_in_enabled=True,
            audio_out_enabled=True,
        ),
        webrtc_connection=runner_args.webrtc_connection,
    )

    # Run your bot logic
    await run_bot(transport)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    from pipecat.runner.run import main
    main()

guides-learn-session-initialization-007.text: Then start your bot with different connection types:

guides-learn-session-initialization-008.text:
# P2P WebRTC (opens browser interface)
python bot.py -t webrtc

# Daily room-based WebRTC
python bot.py -t daily

# Telephony (requires ngrok or similar)
python bot.py -t twilio -x your_domain.ngrok.io

guides-learn-session-initialization-009.text:
where -t specifies the transport type (e.g., webrtc , daily , twilio ) and -x is the optional proxy domain for telephony.

The development runner automatically:

guides-learn-session-initialization-010.text:
- Creates the FastAPI server
- Sets up the appropriate endpoints
- Handles connection management
- Starts your bot instances
- Provides a web interface (for WebRTC)

guides-learn-session-initialization-011.text:
Learn more about building with the development runner in the
runner
guide
.

guides-learn-session-initialization-012.text: While the development runner handles the complexity, understanding the three connection patterns helps you choose the right approach and debug issues:

guides-learn-session-initialization-014.text:
1. Runner serves a web interface at http://localhost:7860/client
2. When you open the page and connect, browser creates a WebRTC offer
3. Runner receives the offer, establishes connection, starts your bot
4. Browser and bot communicate directly via WebRTC

guides-learn-session-initialization-015.text: When to use: Direct client connections, embedded applications, local development

guides-learn-session-initialization-017.text:
1
Room Request
User visits the client application and clicks to start a session
2
Room Creation
Runner calls Daily’s API to create a room and tokens using
pipecat.runner.daily.configure()
3
Parallel Join
Both user’s browser and your bot join the same Daily room
4
Media Handshake
Once media streams are established, browser sends
client_ready
message
5
Bot Activation
Your bot receives the event and starts the conversation

guides-learn-session-initialization-018.text: When to use: Video calls, group sessions, production deployments

guides-learn-session-initialization-019.text:
Room-based WebRTC can also be used for SIP or PSTN connections, which require
different connection patterns. Refer to the
telephony
guide
for details.

guides-learn-session-initialization-021.text:
1. Telephony provider (Twilio, etc.) receives a phone call
2. Provider connects to your runner’s webhook endpoint
3. Runner accepts WebSocket connection and parses telephony-specific messages
4. Your bot starts immediately with the parsed connection data

guides-learn-session-initialization-022.text: When to use: Phone bots, telephony integrations

guides-learn-session-initialization-023.text: How and when your bot begins talking depends on the connection type:

guides-learn-session-initialization-024.text: These connections are ready immediately, so you can start talking right after connection:

guides-learn-session-initialization-026.text: For client/server applications using room-based WebRTC, a handshake ensures both sides are ready and the client won’t miss the opening message:

guides-learn-session-initialization-027.text:
@rtvi.event_handler("on_client_ready")
async def on_client_ready(rtvi):
    await rtvi.set_bot_ready()  # Confirm readiness to client
    # Start the conversation
    await task.queue_frames([LLMRunFrame()])

guides-learn-session-initialization-028.text:
For client/server room-based connections, waiting for
on_client_ready
is
crucial - starting too early can cause the client to miss part of the initial
message.

guides-learn-session-initialization-029.text: Each session runs its own dedicated bot instance for:

guides-learn-session-initialization-030.text:
- Resource Management : Dedicated CPU and memory per session
- Error Isolation : One session crash doesn’t affect others
- Clean Cleanup : Resources automatically freed when sessions end

guides-learn-session-initialization-031.text: The development runner handles this process management automatically.

guides-learn-session-initialization-032.h2: Custom Runners: When You Need More Control
guides-learn-session-initialization-032.text:
The development runner works for most cases, but sometimes you need custom behavior - specific authentication, custom endpoints, or integration with existing systems. For these cases, you can create your own FastAPI runner.

The development runner source code ( available on GitHub ) provides excellent examples for:

Daily Integration Example:

guides-learn-session-initialization-033.h2: Custom Runners: When You Need More Control
guides-learn-session-initialization-033.text:
from pipecat.runner.daily import configure

@app.post("/start")
async def start_bot(background_tasks: BackgroundTasks):
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        room_url, token = await configure(session)

        # Start bot instance
        background_tasks.add_task(run_bot, room_url, token)

        return {"room_url": room_url, "token": token}

guides-learn-session-initialization-034.h2: Custom Runners: When You Need More Control

guides-learn-session-initialization-035.h2: Custom Runners: When You Need More Control
guides-learn-session-initialization-035.text:
from pipecat.runner.utils import parse_telephony_websocket

@app.websocket("/ws")
async def websocket_endpoint(websocket: WebSocket):
    await websocket.accept()

    # Parse provider-specific messages
    transport_type, call_data = await parse_telephony_websocket(websocket)

    # Start bot with parsed data
    await run_telephony_bot(websocket, transport_type, call_data)

guides-learn-session-initialization-036.h2: Custom Runners: When You Need More Control
guides-learn-session-initialization-036.text:
Refer to the development runner source code to understand these patterns
before building custom runners. It handles many edge cases and provides
battle-tested implementations.

guides-learn-session-initialization-037.text:
- Start with the development runner for fastest development and learning
- Understand connection types to choose the right approach for your use case
- Handle startup timing correctly - immediate start vs. handshake patterns matter
- Plan for process isolation - one bot instance per session is the recommended pattern
- Reference the source code when building custom runners for production

guides-learn-session-initialization-038.text: Now that you understand session initialization, let’s explore the different transport options and how to configure them for your specific needs.

guides-learn-session-initialization-039.text:
Learn how Pipecat’s pipeline architecture orchestrates frame processing for
voice AI applications

guides-learn-speech-input-000.text: A key to natural conversations is properly detecting when the user starts and stops speaking. This is more nuanced than simply detecting audio; a brief pause doesn’t always mean the user is done talking.

guides-learn-speech-input-001.text:
Pipecat uses user turn strategies to determine when user turns start and end. These strategies can use different techniques:

For detecting turn start:

guides-learn-speech-input-002.text:
- Voice Activity Detection (VAD): triggers when speech is detected
- Transcription-based (fallback): triggers when transcription is received but VAD didn’t detect speech
- Minimum words: waits for a minimum number of spoken words before triggering

guides-learn-speech-input-003.text: For detecting turn end (default: Smart Turn):

guides-learn-speech-input-004.text:
- Turn detection model (default): uses AI to understand if the user has finished their thought
- Speech timeout: waits for silence after transcription to determine when the user is done

guides-learn-speech-input-005.text: Custom strategies can also be implemented for specific use cases. By combining these techniques, you can create responsive yet natural conversations that don’t interrupt users mid-sentence or wait too long after they’ve finished.

guides-learn-speech-input-006.text:
VAD is responsible for detecting when a user starts and stops speaking. Pipecat uses the Silero VAD , an open-source model that runs locally on CPU with minimal overhead.

Performance characteristics:

guides-learn-speech-input-007.text:
- Processes 30+ms audio chunks in less than 1ms
- Runs on a single CPU thread
- Minimal system resource impact

guides-learn-speech-input-008.text: VAD is configured through VADParams in the LLMContextAggregatorPair :

guides-learn-speech-input-009.text:
from pipecat.audio.vad.silero import SileroVADAnalyzer
from pipecat.audio.vad.vad_analyzer import VADParams
from pipecat.processors.aggregators.llm_response_universal import (
    LLMContextAggregatorPair,
    LLMUserAggregatorParams,
)

vad_analyzer = SileroVADAnalyzer(
    params=VADParams(
        confidence=0.7,      # Minimum confidence for voice detection
        start_secs=0.2,      # Time to wait before confirming speech start
        stop_secs=0.2,       # Time to wait before confirming speech stop
        min_volume=0.6,      # Minimum volume threshold
    )
)

user_aggregator, assistant_aggregator = LLMContextAggregatorPair(
    context,
    user_params=LLMUserAggregatorParams(vad_analyzer=vad_analyzer)),
)

guides-learn-speech-input-010.text: In the vast number of cases, the default values will work well. Only adjust these parameters if you have specific audio conditions that require it.

guides-learn-speech-input-012.text:
- How long a user must speak before VAD confirms speech has started
- Lower values = more responsive, but may trigger on brief sounds
- Higher values = less sensitive, but may miss quick utterances, like “yes”, “no”, or “ok”

guides-learn-speech-input-014.text:
- How much silence must be detected before confirming speech has stopped
- Critical for turn-taking behavior
- A short value (0.2s) allows STT services to finalize sooner, improving transcription speed

guides-learn-speech-input-016.text:
- Generally work well with defaults
- Only adjust after extensive testing with your specific audio conditions

guides-learn-speech-input-017.text:
Changing confidence and min_volume requires careful profiling to ensure
optimal performance across different audio environments and use cases.

guides-learn-speech-input-018.text: While VAD detects speech vs. silence, it can’t understand linguistic context. A pause doesn’t mean the user is done. User turn strategies interpret VAD signals and transcriptions to determine actual turn boundaries.

guides-learn-speech-input-019.text:
1. Turn Start : When VAD detects speech (or transcription arrives), the start strategy emits UserStartedSpeakingFrame and optionally triggers an interruption
2. Turn End : When the stop strategy determines the user is done, it emits UserStoppedSpeakingFrame

guides-learn-speech-input-020.text:
VAD also emits its own frames (
VADUserStartedSpeakingFrame
,
VADUserStoppedSpeakingFrame
) which indicate raw speech/silence detection.
These are inputs to the turn strategies, not the final turn decisions.

guides-learn-speech-input-021.text:
Turn end detection determines when the user has finished speaking and expects a response:

Smart Turn Model (Default) : Uses an AI model to analyze audio and determine if the user has finished their thought. This is the default turn stop strategy.

guides-learn-speech-input-022.text:
from pipecat.audio.turn.smart_turn.local_smart_turn_v3 import LocalSmartTurnAnalyzerV3
from pipecat.turns.user_stop import TurnAnalyzerUserTurnStopStrategy

stop_strategy = TurnAnalyzerUserTurnStopStrategy(
    turn_analyzer=LocalSmartTurnAnalyzerV3()
)

guides-learn-speech-input-023.text: Speech Timeout : Waits for silence after receiving a transcription. Useful as a simpler alternative to Smart Turn.

guides-learn-speech-input-025.text: Complete reference for start and stop strategies

guides-learn-speech-input-027.text:
Interruptions stop the bot when the user starts speaking. This is controlled by the enable_interruptions parameter on start strategies (enabled by default).

When a user turn starts with interruptions enabled:

guides-learn-speech-input-028.text:
1. Bot immediately stops speaking
2. Pending audio and text is cleared
3. Pipeline ready for new user input

guides-learn-speech-input-031.text:
Keep interruptions enabled (default) for natural conversations. This enables
users to interrupt the bot mid-sentence, just like human conversations.

guides-learn-speech-input-032.text: For most voice AI use cases, the defaults work well:

guides-learn-speech-input-033.text:
from pipecat.audio.vad.silero import SileroVADAnalyzer
from pipecat.processors.aggregators.llm_response_universal import (
    LLMContextAggregatorPair,
    LLMUserAggregatorParams,
)

transport = YourTransport(
    params=TransportParams(),
)

# Default configuration: Smart Turn detection + VAD with stop_secs=0.2
user_aggregator, assistant_aggregator = LLMContextAggregatorPair(
    context,
    user_params=LLMUserAggregatorParams(vad_analyzer=SileroVADAnalyzer()),
)

task = PipelineTask(pipeline)

guides-learn-speech-input-034.text: The defaults use TurnAnalyzerUserTurnStopStrategy with LocalSmartTurnAnalyzerV3 for turn detection and SileroVADAnalyzer with stop_secs=0.2 for voice activity detection.

guides-learn-speech-input-035.text:
- Use local VAD : 150-200ms faster than remote VAD services
- Tune for your use case : Test with real audio conditions
- Monitor CPU usage : VAD adds minimal overhead but monitor in production
- Consider turn detection : Improves conversation quality but adds complexity

guides-learn-speech-input-036.text:
- VAD detects speech activity but turn detection understands conversation context
- Configuration affects user experience - tune parameters for your specific use case
- System frames coordinate behavior - enable interruptions and natural turn-taking
- Local processing is faster - Silero VAD provides low-latency speech detection
- Turn detection improves quality - but requires careful VAD configuration

guides-learn-speech-input-037.text: Now that you understand how speech input is detected and processed, let’s explore how that audio gets converted to text through speech recognition.

guides-learn-speech-input-038.text: Learn how to configure speech recognition in your voice AI pipeline

guides-learn-speech-to-text-000.text: Speech to Text (STT) services are responsible for converting user audio into text transcriptions. They receive audio input from users and provide real-time transcriptions that your bot can process and respond to.

guides-learn-speech-to-text-001.text: STT processors must be positioned correctly in your pipeline to receive and process audio frames:

guides-learn-speech-to-text-002.text:
pipeline = Pipeline([
    transport.input(),             # Creates InputAudioRawFrames
    stt,                           # Processes audio → creates TranscriptionFrames
    context_aggregator.user(),     # Uses transcriptions for context
    llm,
    tts,
    transport.output(),
])

guides-learn-speech-to-text-004.text:
- After transport.input() : STT needs InputAudioRawFrame s from the transport
- Before context processing : Transcriptions must be available for context aggregation
- Before LLM processing : Text must be ready for language model input

guides-learn-speech-to-text-005.text: Pipecat provides two types of STT services based on how they process audio:

guides-learn-speech-to-text-007.text:
- Establishes a WebSocket connection to the STT provider
- Continuously streams audio for real-time transcription
- Lower latency due to persistent connection

guides-learn-speech-to-text-009.text:
- Uses local VAD (Voice Activity Detection) to chunk speech
- Sends audio segments to STT service as wav files
- Higher latency due to segmentation and HTTP POST requests

guides-learn-speech-to-text-010.text:
STT services are modular and can be swapped out with no additional overhead.
You can easily switch between streaming and segmented services based on your
needs.

guides-learn-speech-to-text-011.text: Pipecat supports a wide range of STT providers to fit different needs and budgets:

guides-learn-speech-to-text-012.text:
View the complete list of supported speech-to-text providers

Popular options include:

guides-learn-speech-to-text-013.text: Fast, accurate streaming STT with excellent real-time performance

guides-learn-speech-to-text-014.text: Advanced speech recognition with strong accent and dialect handling

guides-learn-speech-to-text-015.text: AI-powered transcription with speaker diarization and sentiment analysis

guides-learn-speech-to-text-016.text: High-performance STT with multilingual support and custom models

guides-learn-speech-to-text-017.text: Microsoft’s enterprise-grade STT service with extensive language support

guides-learn-speech-to-text-018.text: Reliable transcription with strong language model integration

guides-learn-speech-to-text-019.text: Each STT service has its own customization options. Refer to specific service documentation for details:

guides-learn-speech-to-text-020.text:
Explore configuration options for each supported STT provider

For example, let’s look at configuring the DeepgramSTTService using the LiveOptions class:

guides-learn-speech-to-text-021.text:
from deepgram import LiveOptions
from pipecat.services.deepgram.stt import DeepgramSTTService
from pipecat.transcriptions.language import Language

# Configure using LiveOptions for full control
live_options = LiveOptions(
    model="nova-2",
    language=Language.EN_US,
    interim_results=True,        # Enable interim transcripts
    punctuate=True,              # Add punctuation
    profanity_filter=True,       # Filter profanity
    vad_events=False,            # Use pipeline VAD instead
)

stt = DeepgramSTTService(
    api_key=os.getenv("DEEPGRAM_API_KEY"),
    live_options=live_options,
)

guides-learn-speech-to-text-022.text: All STT services inherit from the STTService base class. The base class has base configuration options which are set with smart defaults:

guides-learn-speech-to-text-023.text:
stt = YourSTTService(
    # Service-specific options...
    audio_passthrough=True,      # Pass audio frames downstream (recommended)
    sample_rate=16000,           # Audio sample rate (better set in PipelineParams)
)

guides-learn-speech-to-text-025.text:
- audio_passthrough=True : Allows audio frames to continue downstream to other processors (like audio recording)
- sample_rate : Audio sampling rate - best practice is to set the audio_in_sample_rate in PipelineParams for consistency

guides-learn-speech-to-text-026.text:
Setting
audio_passthrough=False
will stop audio frames from being passed
downstream, which may break audio recording or other audio-dependent
processors.

guides-learn-speech-to-text-027.text: Instead of setting sample rates on individual services, configure them pipeline-wide:

guides-learn-speech-to-text-028.text:
task = PipelineTask(
    pipeline,
    params=PipelineParams(
        audio_in_sample_rate=16000,   # All input processors use this rate
        audio_out_sample_rate=24000,  # All output processors use this rate
    ),
)

guides-learn-speech-to-text-029.text: This ensures all audio processors use consistent sample rates without manual configuration.

guides-learn-speech-to-text-030.text:
Always set audio sample rates in
PipelineParams
to avoid mismatches between
different audio processors. This simplifies configuration and ensures
consistent audio quality across your pipeline.

guides-learn-speech-to-text-031.text: When available, enable interim transcripts for better user experience:

guides-learn-speech-to-text-034.text:
- Notifies context aggregation that more text is coming
- Prevents premature LLM completions
- Enables interruption detection
- Improves conversation flow

guides-learn-speech-to-text-035.text: Use punctuation when available for better LLM comprehension:

guides-learn-speech-to-text-036.text:
stt = DeepgramSTTService(
    api_key=os.getenv("DEEPGRAM_API_KEY"),
    live_options=LiveOptions(
        punctuate=True,        # Adds punctuation
        profanity_filter=True, # Optional content filtering
    )
)

guides-learn-speech-to-text-038.text:
- Professional-looking transcripts
- Better LLM comprehension
- Eliminates post-processing needs
- Improved context understanding

guides-learn-speech-to-text-039.text: While many STT services provide Voice Activity Detection, use Pipecat’s local Silero VAD for better performance:

guides-learn-speech-to-text-040.text:
from pipecat.audio.vad.silero import SileroVADAnalyzer

# Configure in context aggregator
user_aggregator, assistant_aggregator = LLMContextAggregatorPair(
    context,
    user_params=LLMUserAggregatorParams(
        vad_analyzer=SileroVADAnalyzer(),
    ),
)

guides-learn-speech-to-text-042.text:
- 150-200ms faster speech detection (no network round trip)
- More responsive conversation flow
- Better interruption handling
- Reduced latency overall

guides-learn-speech-to-text-043.text: Each STT service has a measured P99 latency for delivering final transcripts after the user stops speaking. This value is used by turn stop strategies to decide how long to wait before ending the user’s turn. If you notice the bot responding too early (cutting off the user) or too late (long pauses), tuning this value can help.

guides-learn-speech-to-text-044.text:
Learn about TTFS latency, see default values for every STT service, and how to
measure and override for your deployment

guides-learn-speech-to-text-045.text:
