- Pipeline placement matters - STT must come after transport input, before context processing
- Service types differ - streaming services have lower latency than segmented
- Services are modular - easily swap providers without code changes
- Best practices improve performance - use interim results, formatting, and local VAD
- Configuration affects quality - proper setup significantly impacts transcription accuracy

guides-learn-speech-to-text-046.text: Now that you understand speech recognition, let’s explore how to manage conversation context and memory in your voice AI bot.

guides-learn-speech-to-text-047.text: Learn how to handle conversation history and context in your pipeline

guides-learn-text-to-speech-000.text: Text to Speech (TTS) services are responsible for converting text into natural-sounding speech audio. They receive text input from LLMs and other sources, then generate audio output that users can hear through their connected devices.

guides-learn-text-to-speech-001.text: TTS processors must be positioned correctly in your pipeline to receive text and generate audio frames:

guides-learn-text-to-speech-002.text:
pipeline = Pipeline([
    transport.input(),
    stt,
    context_aggregator.user(),
    llm,                           # Generates LLMTextFrames
    tts,                           # Processes text → creates TTSAudioRawFrames
    transport.output(),            # Sends audio to user
    context_aggregator.assistant(), # Processes TTSTextFrames for context
])

guides-learn-text-to-speech-004.text:
- After LLM processing : TTS needs LLMTextFrame s from language model responses
- Before transport output : Audio must be generated before sending to user
- Before assistant context aggregator : Ensures spoken text is captured in conversation history

guides-learn-text-to-speech-005.text: TTS generates speech through two primary mechanisms:

guides-learn-text-to-speech-006.text:
1. Streamed LLM tokens via LLMTextFrame s:
  - By default, TTS aggregates streaming tokens into complete sentences before synthesis ( TextAggregationMode.SENTENCE )
  - Set text_aggregation_mode=TextAggregationMode.TOKEN to stream tokens directly for lower latency
  - Audio bytes stream back and play immediately
  - End-to-end latency often under 200ms
2. Direct speech requests via TTSSpeakFrame s:
  - Bypasses LLM for immediate audio generation
  - Optionally appends text to conversation context via append_to_context parameter
  - Useful for system messages, greetings, or injected speech

guides-learn-text-to-speech-008.text:
- TTSAudioRawFrame s: Raw audio data for playback
- TTSTextFrame s: Text that was actually spoken (for context updates)
- TTSStartedFrame / TTSStoppedFrame : Speech boundary markers

guides-learn-text-to-speech-009.text: Pipecat supports a wide range of TTS providers with different capabilities and performance characteristics:

guides-learn-text-to-speech-010.text: View the complete list of supported text-to-speech providers

guides-learn-text-to-speech-012.text:
- Cartesia : Ultra-low latency with word timestamps
- ElevenLabs : High-quality voices with emotion control
- Rime : Ultra-realistic voices with advanced features

guides-learn-text-to-speech-014.text:
- OpenAI TTS : High-quality synthesis with multiple voices
- Azure Speech : Enterprise-grade with extensive language support
- Google Text-to-Speech : Reliable with WaveNet voices

guides-learn-text-to-speech-016.text:
- Word timestamps : Enable word-level accuracy for context and subtitles
- Voice cloning : Custom voice creation from samples
- Emotion control : Dynamic emotional expression
- SSML support : Fine-grained pronunciation control

guides-learn-text-to-speech-017.text:
WebSocket services typically provide the lowest latency, while HTTP services
may have intermittent higher latency due to their request/response nature.

guides-learn-text-to-speech-018.text: Each TTS service has its own configuration options. Here’s an example with Cartesia:

guides-learn-text-to-speech-019.text:
from pipecat.services.cartesia.tts import CartesiaTTSService
from pipecat.transcriptions.language import Language

tts = CartesiaTTSService(
    api_key=os.getenv("CARTESIA_API_KEY"),
    settings=CartesiaTTSService.Settings(
        model="sonic-3",
        voice="voice-id-here",
        language=Language.EN,     # Speech language
    ),
    # Word timestamps automatically enabled for precise context updates
)

guides-learn-text-to-speech-020.text: Word timestamps: Services like Cartesia, ElevenLabs, and Rime provide word-level timestamps that enable precise context updates during interruptions and better synchronization with other pipeline components. For example, if an interruption occurs while the bot is speaking, the word timestamps allow you to accurately capture which words were spoken up to that point, enabling better context management and user experience. Additionally, transcription events streamed from server to client can be done in sync with the audio output, allowing for real-time subtitles or captions.

guides-learn-text-to-speech-021.text: Explore configuration options for each supported TTS provider

guides-learn-text-to-speech-022.text: Set consistent audio settings across your entire pipeline:

guides-learn-text-to-speech-023.text:
task = PipelineTask(
    pipeline,
    params=PipelineParams(
        audio_in_sample_rate=16000,   # Input audio quality
        audio_out_sample_rate=24000,  # Output audio quality (TTS)
    ),
)

guides-learn-text-to-speech-024.text:
Set the
audio_out_sample_rate
to match your TTS service’s requirements for
optimal quality. This is preferred to setting the sample_rate directly in the
TTS service as the PipelineParam ensures that all output sample_rates match.

guides-learn-text-to-speech-025.text: By default, TTS services have a built-in text aggregator that collects streaming text into sentences before passing them to the underlying service. However, you can customize this behavior by inserting an LLMTextProcessor with a different text aggregator before the TTS in your pipeline. This allows the ability to categorize and structure text into logical units beyond simple sentences, such as code blocks, URLs, or custom tags. You can then configure the TTS to handle these different text types appropriately, such as skipping code blocks or transforming them in a just-in-time manner before speaking.

guides-learn-text-to-speech-026.text: To skip certain text aggregations (e.g., code snippets or URLs) and keep them from being spoken, use a custom text aggregator like PatternPairAggregator within an LLMTextProcessor , and configure it to identify and handle specific patterns in the text stream. With this, you can then pass any aggregated types you want to skip (like “code”) to the TTS service’s skip_aggregator_types parameter.

guides-learn-text-to-speech-027.text: # Create pattern aggregator pattern_aggregator = PatternPairAggregator() # Add pattern for JSON data pattern_aggregator.add_pattern( type="code", start_pattern="", end_pattern="", action=MatchAction.AGGREGATE ) # Set the aggregator on an LLMTextProcessor llm_text_processor = LLMTextProcessor(text_aggregator=pattern_aggregator) # Initialize TTS service, and don't speak JSON data tts = CartesiaTTSService( api_key=os.getenv("CARTESIA_API_KEY"), skip_aggregator_types=["code"], # The strings here should match the types defined in the PatternPairAggregator ) # add the llm_text_processor to your pipeline after the llm and before the tts # llm -> llm_text_processor -> tts

guides-learn-text-to-speech-028.text:
For TTS-specific text preprocessing, you can provide custom text transforms that modify text in a just-in-time manner before sending the text off to the TTS service. This is useful for handling special text segments that need to be altered for better pronunciation or clarity, such as spelling out phone numbers, removing URLs, or expanding abbreviations. These text transforms can be mapped to a specific text aggregation type, like with skip_aggregator_types , or applied globally to all text using '*' as the type.

Text transforms are registered directly on the TTS service instance via the add_text_transformer() method or during initialization using the text_transforms parameter.

guides-learn-text-to-speech-029.text:
The intentions of text transforms are meant to be TTS-specific modifications
that do not affect the underlying LLM text or context. That said, since the
context aggregator attempts to base its context on what was actually spoken,
for services that support word timestamps, like Cartesia, ElevenLabs, and
Rime,these transforms will modify the context as they modify what is spoken.

guides-learn-text-to-speech-030.text:
# Create pattern aggregator
pattern_aggregator = PatternPairAggregator()

# Add patterns for different parts of an explanation
pattern_aggregator.add_pattern(
    type="phone_number",
    start_pattern="<pnum>",
    end_pattern="</pnum>",
    action=MatchAction.AGGREGATE
)

# Set the aggregator on an LLMTextProcessor
llm_text_processor = LLMTextProcessor(text_aggregator=pattern_aggregator)

# Text-to-Speech service
tts = CartesiaTTSService(
    api_key=os.getenv("CARTESIA_API_KEY"),
)

# Text transformers for TTS
# This will insert Cartesia's spell tags around the provided text.
async def spell_out_text(text: str, type: str) -> str:
    # CartesiaTTSService provides a helper for this along with other common transforms
    return CartesiaTTSService.SPELL(text)

async def replace_acronyms(text: str, type: str) -> str:
    # Replace "SEC" with "Southeastern Conference"
    return text.replace(" SEC ", " Southeastern Conference ")

# Setup the text transformers in TTS to spell out phone numbers and replace
# acronyms. The string below matches the type defined in the PatternPairAggregator
# above so that whenever those segments are encountered, this transform
# is applied
tts.add_text_transformer(spell_out_text, "phone_number")
tts.add_text_transformer(replace_acronyms, "*")  # Apply to all text

# add the llm_text_processor to your pipeline after the llm and before the tts
#   llm -> llm_text_processor -> tts

guides-learn-text-to-speech-031.text:
Text filters are no longer the preferred method for text preprocessing and
will be deprecated in future releases. Instead, you should use one of the
methods described above.

guides-learn-text-to-speech-032.text: Apply preprocessing to text before synthesis:

guides-learn-text-to-speech-033.text:
from pipecat.utils.text.markdown_text_filter import MarkdownTextFilter

tts = YourTTSService(
    # ... other options
    text_filters=[
        MarkdownTextFilter(),     # Remove markdown formatting
        CustomTextFilter(),       # Your custom processing
    ],
)

guides-learn-text-to-speech-035.text:
- MarkdownTextFilter : Strips markdown formatting from LLM responses
- Custom filters : Implement your own text preprocessing logic

guides-learn-text-to-speech-036.text:
Sometimes you want text from the LLM to flow through the pipeline—updating the conversation context, reaching observers, or being processed by custom frame processors—without being spoken by the TTS service. Pipecat provides a skip_tts attribute on text and response frames for this purpose.

When skip_tts is True on a frame, the TTS service passes it through without generating audio, but the text still reaches downstream processors like the assistant context aggregator.

guides-learn-text-to-speech-037.text: Use LLMConfigureOutputFrame to tell the LLM service to mark all subsequent output frames ( LLMTextFrame , LLMFullResponseStartFrame , LLMFullResponseEndFrame ) with skip_tts :

guides-learn-text-to-speech-038.text:
from pipecat.frames.frames import LLMConfigureOutputFrame

# Tell the LLM to skip TTS for all output
await task.queue_frame(LLMConfigureOutputFrame(skip_tts=True))

# ... LLM responses will not be spoken ...

# Re-enable TTS
await task.queue_frame(LLMConfigureOutputFrame(skip_tts=False))

guides-learn-text-to-speech-039.text: This is useful when you want to toggle TTS on or off for an entire stretch of conversation, such as switching between voice and text input modes.

guides-learn-text-to-speech-040.text: For more granular control, set skip_tts=True directly on individual text frames. This is useful when building custom frame processors that selectively silence certain parts of the LLM output:

guides-learn-text-to-speech-041.text:
from pipecat.frames.frames import LLMTextFrame

# In a custom frame processor
frame = LLMTextFrame(text)
frame.skip_tts = True
await self.push_frame(frame)

guides-learn-text-to-speech-042.text:
The
skip_tts
attribute is available on
TextFrame
and all its subclasses
(
LLMTextFrame
,
AggregatedTextFrame
,
TTSTextFrame
, etc.), as well as
LLMFullResponseStartFrame
and
LLMFullResponseEndFrame
.

guides-learn-text-to-speech-043.text:
Encoding structured output from the LLM. You can instruct the LLM to include markers or metadata in its response that should be processed by pipeline logic but not spoken. For example, Pipecat’s turn completion detection uses this approach — the LLM outputs completion markers ( ✓ , ○ , ◐ ) that are pushed with skip_tts=True so they update the context but aren’t spoken.

Switching between voice and text input. When a client sends text input instead of speech, you may want the bot to respond with text only. The client SDKs support this via sendText() with audio_response: false , which uses LLMConfigureOutputFrame internally to temporarily disable TTS for that response.

Testing without audio. When building test pipelines, you can use LLMConfigureOutputFrame(skip_tts=True) to bypass audio generation entirely while still exercising the rest of the pipeline.

guides-learn-text-to-speech-044.text: Use TTSSpeakFrame for immediate speech synthesis:

guides-learn-text-to-speech-045.text:
from pipecat.frames.frames import TTSSpeakFrame

# Make bot speak directly
await tts.queue_frame(TTSSpeakFrame("Hello, how can I help you?"))

# Append spoken text to conversation context
await tts.queue_frame(
    TTSSpeakFrame("Welcome! Let's begin.", append_to_context=True)
)

# Speak without adding to context
await tts.queue_frame(
    TTSSpeakFrame("Processing...", append_to_context=False)
)

guides-learn-text-to-speech-046.text: The append_to_context parameter controls whether the spoken text is added to the conversation history. When append_to_context=True , the text is automatically committed to the context after being spoken, making it useful for bot greetings and injected speech that should be part of the conversation flow.

guides-learn-text-to-speech-047.text: Update TTS settings during conversation using typed settings objects:

guides-learn-text-to-speech-048.text:
from pipecat.frames.frames import TTSUpdateSettingsFrame
from pipecat.services.cartesia.tts import CartesiaTTSSettings

# Change voice speed during conversation
await task.queue_frames([
    TTSUpdateSettingsFrame(delta=CartesiaTTSSettings(speed="fast")),
    TTSSpeakFrame("I'm speaking faster now!")
])

guides-learn-text-to-speech-049.text:
- Pipeline placement matters - TTS must come after LLM, before transport output
- Service types differ - WebSocket services provide lower latency than HTTP
- Text processing affects quality - use aggregation and filters for better results
- Word timestamps enable precision - better interruption handling and context accuracy
- Configuration impacts performance - balance quality, latency, and bandwidth needs
- Services are modular - easily swap providers without changing pipeline code

guides-learn-text-to-speech-050.text: You’ve now learned how to build a complete voice AI pipeline! Let’s explore some additional topics to enhance your implementation.

guides-learn-text-to-speech-051.text: Learn how to terminate your voice AI pipeline at the end of a conversation

guides-learn-transports-000.text: Transports are the communication layer between users and your Pipecat bot. They handle receiving and sending audio, video, and data, serving as the media interface that enables real-time interaction.

guides-learn-transports-001.text: Pipecat supports multiple transport types to fit different use cases and deployment scenarios:

guides-learn-transports-002.text:
WebRTC-based transport using Daily’s infrastructure for video calls and
conferencing

guides-learn-transports-003.text: WebSocket transport for telephony providers and custom WebSocket connections

guides-learn-transports-004.text: Specialized transport for HeyGen LiveAvatar video generation and streaming

guides-learn-transports-005.text: WebRTC transport using LiveKit’s real-time communication platform

guides-learn-transports-006.text: Direct peer-to-peer WebRTC connections without cloud infrastructure

guides-learn-transports-007.text: Specialized transport for Tavus video generation and streaming

guides-learn-transports-008.text: General-purpose WebSocket transport for custom implementations

guides-learn-transports-009.text: Transports provide two key components for your pipeline: input() and output() methods. These methods define how the transport interacts with the pipeline:

guides-learn-transports-010.text:
pipeline = Pipeline([
    transport.input(),              # Receives user audio/video
    stt,
    context_aggregator.user(),
    llm,
    tts,
    transport.output(),             # Sends bot audio/video
    context_aggregator.assistant(), # Processes after output
])

guides-learn-transports-012.text:
- transport.input() typically goes first in the pipeline to receive user input
- transport.output() doesn’t always go last - you may want processors after it
- Post-output processing enables synchronized actions like:
  - Recording with word-level accuracy
  - Displaying subtitles synchronized to audio
  - Capturing context information precisely timed to output

guides-learn-transports-013.text: Transports are modular components in your Pipeline, allowing you to flexibly change how users connect to your bot depending on the context. This modularity enables you to:

guides-learn-transports-014.text:
- Switch environments easily : Use P2P WebRTC for development, Daily for production
- Support multiple connection types : Same bot logic works across different transports
- Optimize for use case : Choose the best transport for your specific requirements

guides-learn-transports-015.text: All transports are configured using TransportParams , which provides common settings across transport types:

guides-learn-transports-016.text:
from pipecat.transports.base_transport import TransportParams

params = TransportParams(
    # Audio settings
    audio_in_enabled=True,
    audio_out_enabled=True,

    # Video settings
    video_in_enabled=False,
    video_out_enabled=False,

    # Video stream configuration
    video_out_width=1024,
    video_out_height=576,
    video_out_bitrate=800000,
    video_out_framerate=30,
)

guides-learn-transports-017.text:
Each transport may have its own specialized parameters class that extends
TransportParams with transport-specific options. Check the individual
transport documentation for details.

guides-learn-transports-018.text:
For advanced turn detection (like Smart Turn), configure
User Turn
Strategies
on the
context aggregator instead of using the transport’s turn_analyzer parameter.

guides-learn-transports-019.text: Complete reference for all transport configuration options

guides-learn-transports-020.text: Telephony services (phone calls) use WebSocket connections with specialized serialization:

guides-learn-transports-021.text: Media Streams over WebSocket with TwilioFrameSerializer

guides-learn-transports-025.text: Telephony requires a FrameSerializer to handle provider-specific message formats:

guides-learn-transports-026.text:
# Create provider-specific serializer
serializer = TwilioFrameSerializer(
    stream_sid=stream_sid,
    call_sid=call_sid,
    account_sid=os.getenv("TWILIO_ACCOUNT_SID", ""),
    auth_token=os.getenv("TWILIO_AUTH_TOKEN", ""),
)

# Configure transport with serializer
transport = FastAPIWebsocketTransport(
    websocket=websocket_client,
    params=FastAPIWebsocketParams(
        audio_in_enabled=True,
        audio_out_enabled=True,
        add_wav_header=False,
        serializer=serializer,  # Provider-specific serialization
    ),
)

guides-learn-transports-027.text:
The development runner automatically detects and configures the appropriate
serializer when using
parse_telephony_websocket()
.

guides-learn-transports-028.text: The development runner provides a pattern for conditionally selecting transports based on the environment:

guides-learn-transports-029.text:
async def bot(runner_args: RunnerArguments):
    """Main bot entry point compatible with Pipecat Cloud."""

    transport = None

    if isinstance(runner_args, DailyRunnerArguments):
        from pipecat.transports.daily.transport import DailyParams, DailyTransport

        transport = DailyTransport(
            runner_args.room_url,
            runner_args.token,
            "Pipecat Bot",
            params=DailyParams(
                audio_in_enabled=True,
                audio_out_enabled=True,
            ),
        )

    elif isinstance(runner_args, SmallWebRTCRunnerArguments):
        from pipecat.transports.base_transport import TransportParams
        from pipecat.transports.network.small_webrtc import SmallWebRTCTransport

        transport = SmallWebRTCTransport(
            params=TransportParams(
                audio_in_enabled=True,
                audio_out_enabled=True,
            ),
            webrtc_connection=runner_args.webrtc_connection,
        )
    else:
        logger.error(f"Unsupported runner arguments type: {type(runner_args)}")
        return

    if transport is None:
        logger.error("Failed to create transport")
        return

    await run_bot(transport)

guides-learn-transports-030.text: This pattern allows you to run the same bot code across different environments with different connection types.

guides-learn-transports-031.text: Understanding when to use each connection type is crucial for building effective voice AI applications:

guides-learn-transports-032.text:
Best for: Browser apps, mobile apps, real-time conversations

Advantages:

guides-learn-transports-033.text:
- Low latency : Optimized for real-time media with minimal delay
- Built-in resilience : Handles packet loss and network variations
- Advanced audio processing : Echo cancellation, noise reduction, automatic gain control
- Quality monitoring : Detailed performance and media quality statistics
- Automatic timestamping : Simplifies interruption and playout logic
- Robust reconnection : Built-in connection management

guides-learn-transports-035.text:
- Building client-facing applications (web, mobile)
- Conversational latency is critical
- Users are on potentially unreliable networks
- You need built-in audio processing features

guides-learn-transports-036.text:
Best for: Telephony integration, server-to-server communication, prototyping

Limitations for real-time media:

guides-learn-transports-037.text:
- TCP-based : Subject to head-of-line blocking
- Network sensitivity : Less resilient to packet loss and jitter
- Manual implementation : Requires custom logic for reconnection, timestamping
- Limited observability : Harder to monitor connection quality

guides-learn-transports-039.text:
- Integrating with telephony providers (Twilio, Telnyx, etc.)
- Building server-to-server connections
- Prototyping or latency isn’t critical
- Working within existing WebSocket infrastructure

guides-learn-transports-040.text:
- Transports are modular - swap them without changing bot logic
- Choose based on use case - WebRTC for clients, WebSocket for telephony
- Configuration is standardized - TransportParams work across transport types
- Pipeline placement matters - consider what processing happens after output
- Development runner helps - provides patterns for multi-transport bots

guides-learn-transports-041.text: Now that you understand how transports connect users to your bot, let’s explore how to configure speech recognition to convert user audio into text.

guides-learn-transports-042.text: Learn how to configure speech recognition in your voice AI pipeline

guides-learn-whats-next-000.text: Congratulations! You’ve learned how to build complete voice AI applications with Pipecat. You’re now well on your way to understanding pipelines, processors, transports, and all the components needed to create sophisticated conversational AI.

guides-learn-whats-next-001.text:
Production-ready applications - Dive into 30+ complete examples including
multimodal bots, creative applications, and enterprise integrations.

guides-learn-whats-next-002.text:
Master advanced features - Explore specialized topics like telephony,
deployment, custom processors, and production optimization.

guides-learn-whats-next-003.text: Get involved with the Pipecat community to share your projects, get support, and contribute to the ecosystem:

guides-learn-whats-next-004.text:

- GitHub : Contribute to the project and report issues

guides-telephony-daily-phone-numbers-000.text: Use Daily’s REST API to find, buy, list, and release phone numbers programmatically.

guides-telephony-daily-phone-numbers-001.text:
You can also purchase phone numbers through the Pipecat Cloud Dashboard by
going to
Settings
>
Telephony
and following the UI.

guides-telephony-daily-phone-numbers-002.text: We offer several search filters , such as, region , city , areacode , etc that can help narrow down the search space for a phone number. The response contains phone numbers that meet the chosen criteria. For simplicty, in the below examples, we will use +19499870006 as an example phone_number .

guides-telephony-daily-phone-numbers-004.text: To purchase the chosen number, make a POST request to the buy-phone-number and pass the chosen phone number in the data field. The response returns the phone number and a unique id. The unique id is needed when releasing the chosen phone number, for example.

guides-telephony-daily-phone-numbers-006.text:
Use the id to select the desired phone number for dial-out. Pass it as
callerId
in the
start_dialout
API or include it in the
dialout_settings
JSON.

guides-telephony-daily-phone-numbers-007.text: To release a phone number associated with your account. Successfully deleting the number takes the phone number out of operation.

guides-telephony-daily-phone-numbers-008.text:
For compliance reasons, a phone number cannot be released until 14 days after
purchase. A successful release operation cannot be undone, and the phone
number will be taken out of operation.

guides-telephony-daily-phone-numbers-010.text: You can also list all your purchased phone numbers using the following API

guides-telephony-daily-phone-numbers-012.text:
Please contact help@daily.co for adding Caller Name (CNAM) to the purchased phone numbers. Daily will need your Name or Company Name to register with the United States CNAM Registry. Once registered, the Caller ID will display the appropriate name on the calling party’s device.

We need the following information:

guides-telephony-daily-phone-numbers-013.text:
- All phone numbers and the corresponding CNAM entry. A CNAM entry can only be 14 letter long, including spaces.
- Company documents
- An employee’s ID/driver’s license

guides-telephony-daily-phone-numbers-014.text:
It can take up to 48 hours for the CNAM to reflect on the national caller ID
name database and can take longer to update for major carriers, who check the
national database periodically.

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-000.text:
- An active Daily developer account with API key
- At least one phone number purchased through Daily (covered below)
- For local development: ngrok to expose your bot to the Internet

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-001.text: Complete dial-in implementation with the Pipecat development runner

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-002.text: Outbound calling with Daily PSTN integration

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-003.text: Cold transfer implementation for advanced call routing

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-004.text: Before setting up dial-in or dial-out, you’ll need to purchase phone numbers through Daily.

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-005.text:
Complete guide to searching, purchasing, and managing phone numbers with
Daily’s REST API

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-006.text: Dial-in allows users to call your phone number and connect directly to your Pipecat bot.

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-007.text: Here’s the sequence of events when someone calls your Daily phone number:

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-008.text:
1. Daily receives an incoming call to your phone number
2. Daily calls your webhook endpoint
3. The webhook creates a Daily room with SIP configuration
4. The webhook starts your bot with the room details and caller information
5. The caller is put on hold with music
6. The bot joins the Daily room and signals readiness
7. Daily forwards the call to the Daily room
8. The caller and bot are connected for the conversation

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-009.text:
The Pipecat development runner provides built-in webhook handling for Daily PSTN dial-in, eliminating the need for a separate webhook server.

**​1. Run your bot with dial-in support**

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-011.text:
This starts a FastAPI server on port 7860 with a /daily-dialin-webhook endpoint.

**​2. Expose your bot to the internet**

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-014.text:
Use
ngrok http 7860 --subdomain your-subdomain
for a reusable URL.

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-015.text:
**​3. Configure your Daily phone number**

Set your phone number’s room_creation_api webhook to:

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-017.text: Instructions for configuring the webhook URL for your phone number

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-019.text:
Ensure your
DAILY_API_KEY
has the phone number associated with it. This is
required for the
pinlessCallUpdate
API call that connects the caller to the
room.

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-020.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-in
guides-telephony-daily-pstn-020.text: The bot receives dial-in information through RunnerArguments containing:

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-021.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-in
guides-telephony-daily-pstn-021.text:
- room_url : Daily room URL for the call
- token : Daily room token for authentication
- body : Contains DailyDialinRequest with call details

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-022.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-in

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-023.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-in
guides-telephony-daily-pstn-023.text:
from pipecat.runner.types import DailyDialinRequest, RunnerArguments
from pipecat.transports.daily import DailyTransport, DailyParams, DailyDialinSettings

async def bot(runner_args: RunnerArguments):
    # Parse dial-in request from runner
    request = DailyDialinRequest.model_validate(runner_args.body)

    # Configure dial-in settings with webhook data
    daily_dialin_settings = DailyDialinSettings(
        call_id=request.dialin_settings.call_id,
        call_domain=request.dialin_settings.call_domain
    )

    # Create transport with dial-in configuration
    transport = DailyTransport(
        runner_args.room_url,
        runner_args.token,
        "Voice Bot",
        DailyParams(
            api_key=request.daily_api_key,
            api_url=request.daily_api_url,
            dialin_settings=daily_dialin_settings,
            audio_in_enabled=True,
            audio_out_enabled=True,
        )
    )

    # Your bot setup and pipeline creation here...

if __name__ == "__main__":
    from pipecat.runner.run import main
    main()

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-024.text:
See the full bot.py with argument handling, transport setup, and pipeline
configuration

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-025.h3: Customize Your Bot with Caller Information
guides-telephony-daily-pstn-025.text: Use the caller’s phone number to personalize the conversation:

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-026.h3: Customize Your Bot with Caller Information
guides-telephony-daily-pstn-026.text:
async def bot(runner_args: RunnerArguments):
    # Parse dial-in request
    request = DailyDialinRequest.model_validate(runner_args.body)

    # Get caller's phone number
    caller_phone = request.dialin_settings.From

    # Look up customer information from your database
    customer = await get_customer_by_phone(caller_phone)

    # Customize the system prompt
    messages = [
        {
            "role": "system",
            "content": f"You are a helpful assistant for {customer.name}. "
                      f"Their account status is {customer.status}. "
                      "Keep responses concise and conversational."
        }
    ]

    # Use the customized context in your bot...

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-027.text:
See the full README with step-by-step setup, environment variables, and
troubleshooting tips

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-028.text: Dial-out allows your bot to initiate calls to phone numbers. Unlike dial-in, your bot starts the call rather than waiting for incoming calls.

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-029.text:
You must contact Daily to enable dial-out for your account. Submit a support
request
here
.

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-030.text: Here’s the sequence of events for dial-out calls:

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-031.text:
1. Your application triggers a dial-out (via API call or user action)
2. Server creates a Daily room with dial-out capabilities enabled
3. Bot joins the Daily room and sets up the pipeline
4. Bot initiates the dial-out call to the target phone number
5. Daily connects the call to the phone number
6. The recipient answers and is connected to your bot
7. The bot handles the conversation with the called party

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-032.h3: Set up your server for dial-out
guides-telephony-daily-pstn-032.text: The dial-out server is simpler than dial-in since you’re initiating calls rather than receiving webhooks. Your server needs to:

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-033.h3: Set up your server for dial-out
guides-telephony-daily-pstn-033.text:
1. Create Daily rooms with dial-out enabled
2. Start bot processes with the target phone number
3. Handle API requests to trigger outbound calls

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-034.text: See the full FastAPI server code with room creation and dial-out triggering

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-035.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-out
guides-telephony-daily-pstn-035.text:
The dial-out bot receives the target phone number and creates a transport without dial-in settings:

Bot Entry Point : The bot() method receives RunnerArguments containing:

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-036.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-out
guides-telephony-daily-pstn-036.text:
- room_url : Daily room URL for the call
- token : Daily room token for authentication
- phone_number : Target phone number to call

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-037.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-out
guides-telephony-daily-pstn-037.text: Transport Creation : These arguments are used to configure the DailyTransport :

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-038.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-out
guides-telephony-daily-pstn-038.text:
from pipecat.transports.daily import DailyTransport, DailyParams

async def bot(args: RunnerArguments):
    # Create transport for dial-out (no dial-in settings needed)
    transport = DailyTransport(
        args.room_url,
        args.token,
        "Voice Bot",
        DailyParams(
            api_url=os.getenv("DAILY_API_URL", "https://api.daily.co/v1"),
            api_key=os.getenv("DAILY_API_KEY"),
            audio_in_enabled=True,
            audio_out_enabled=True,
            video_out_enabled=False,
            transcription_enabled=True,
        )
    )

    # Start the dial-out call
    await transport.start_dialout(args.phone_number)

    # Pass transport to your bot pipeline
    # Your bot setup and pipeline creation here...

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-043.text: Daily supports cold transfers, allowing you to transfer an active call to another phone number. The bot can initiate a transfer and then leave the call, connecting the caller directly to the transfer destination.

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-044.text:
1. Bot receives transfer request (via function call or user input)
2. Bot informs the caller about the transfer
3. Bot initiates SIP call transfer to the destination number
4. Daily connects the transfer to the destination
5. Bot leaves the call (cold transfer)
6. Caller and destination continue the conversation

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-045.text: Call transfers are typically implemented as LLM function calls that your bot can invoke:

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-046.text:
async def dial_operator(transport: BaseTransport, params: FunctionCallParams):
    """Function the bot can call to transfer to an operator."""
    operator_number = os.getenv("OPERATOR_NUMBER", "+1234567890")

    # Inform the user about the transfer
    content = "I'm transferring you to a supervisor now. Please hold while I connect you."
    message = {"role": "system", "content": content}
    await params.llm.push_frame(LLMMessagesAppendFrame([message], run_llm=True))

    # Execute the SIP call transfer
    transfer_params = {"toEndPoint": operator_number}
    await transport.sip_call_transfer(transfer_params)

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-047.text: Your bot should handle transfer-related events to manage the call flow:

guides-telephony-daily-pstn-048.text:
@transport.event_handler("on_dialout_answered")
async def on_dialout_answered(transport, data):
    logger.info(f"Transfer successful: {data}")
    # Cold transfer: bot leaves, caller and operator continue
    await task.queue_frames([EndFrame()])

guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-003.text: You’ll need Exotel phone numbers for both dial-in and dial-out functionality:

guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-004.text:
- Visit exotel.com and purchase phone numbers
- Complete KYC verification if required
- Ensure voice streaming is enabled on your account (contact Exotel support if needed)

guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-005.text: Configure your environment variables for Exotel and AI services:

guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-006.text:

guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-007.text: Dial-in allows users to call your Exotel number and connect to your Pipecat bot via WebSocket Voice Streaming. Unlike other providers, Exotel automatically provides caller information (to/from numbers) in the WebSocket messages, so no custom server is needed for basic dial-in functionality.

guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-008.text: Here’s the sequence of events when someone calls your Exotel number:

guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-009.text:
1. Exotel receives an incoming call to your phone number
2. Exotel executes your App Bazaar flow which includes a Voicebot applet
3. Exotel opens a WebSocket to your server with real-time audio and call metadata
4. Your bot processes the audio using the Pipecat pipeline
5. The bot responds with audio sent back to Exotel over WebSocket
6. Exotel plays the audio to the caller in real-time

guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-010.h3: Set up your App Bazaar flow
guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-010.text: Exotel uses App Bazaar to configure call flows. For dial-in, you create an app with a Voicebot applet that establishes a WebSocket connection directly to your bot:

guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-011.h3: Set up your App Bazaar flow
guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-011.text:
1. Navigate to App Bazaar in your Exotel dashboard
2. Create a new app or edit an existing one
3. Add a Voicebot applet (not “Stream” or “Passthru”)
4. Configure the WebSocket URL : wss://your-server.com/ws
5. Add a Hangup applet at the end to properly terminate calls

guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-012.h3: Set up your App Bazaar flow
guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-012.text: Your flow should look like: Call Start → [Voicebot Applet] → [Hangup Applet]

guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-013.h3: Personalizing Your Bot with Caller Information
guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-013.text: Exotel automatically includes caller information (to/from numbers) in the WebSocket messages. You can use the caller’s phone number to:

guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-014.h3: Personalizing Your Bot with Caller Information
guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-014.text:
- Look up customer information in your database
- Personalize greetings and responses based on caller identity
- Route calls to different bot behaviors (e.g., VIP handling, support vs sales)
- Implement custom business logic based on the caller

guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-015.h3: Personalizing Your Bot with Caller Information
guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-015.text: The example bot demonstrates how to extract caller information from Exotel’s WebSocket messages and use it to personalize the conversation.

guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-016.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-in
guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-016.text:
Your bot receives the WebSocket connection and automatically gets call information from Exotel’s WebSocket messages. The key components are:

WebSocket Parsing : Pipecat’s built-in parser extracts call data from Exotel’s WebSocket messages:

guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-017.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-in
guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-017.text:
from pipecat.runner.utils import parse_telephony_websocket

async def run_bot(websocket: WebSocket):
    # Parse Exotel WebSocket data
    transport_type, call_data = await parse_telephony_websocket(websocket)

    # Extract call information (automatically provided by Exotel)
    # Data format: {
    #     "stream_id": str,
    #     "call_id": str,
    #     "account_sid": str,
    #     "from": str,
    #     "to": str,
    # }
    stream_id = call_data["stream_id"]
    call_id = call_data["call_id"]  # Exotel's CallSid
    account_sid = call_data["account_sid"]
    from_number = call_data["from"]  # Caller's number
    to_number = call_data["to"]      # Your Exotel number

guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-018.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-in
guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-018.text: Transport Creation : Configure the WebSocket transport with Exotel serialization:

guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-019.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-in
guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-019.text:
# Create Exotel serializer with call details
serializer = ExotelFrameSerializer(
    stream_id=stream_id,
    call_id=call_id,
    account_sid=account_sid,
    api_key=os.getenv("EXOTEL_API_KEY"),
    api_token=os.getenv("EXOTEL_API_TOKEN"),
)

# Configure WebSocket transport
transport = FastAPIWebsocketTransport(
    websocket=websocket,
    params=FastAPIWebsocketParams(
        audio_in_enabled=True,
        audio_out_enabled=True,
        add_wav_header=False,
        serializer=serializer,
    ),
)

# Your bot pipeline setup here...

guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-026.text: Dial-out allows your bot to initiate calls to phone numbers using Exotel’s outbound calling capabilities with WebSocket Voice Streaming.

guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-027.text: Here’s the sequence of events for dial-out calls:

guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-028.text:
1. Your application triggers a dial-out (via API call or user action)
2. Server initiates an Exotel call using the Voice API
3. Exotel establishes the call and opens a WebSocket connection
4. Your bot joins the WebSocket and sets up the pipeline
5. The recipient answers and is connected to your bot
6. The bot handles the conversation with real-time audio streaming

guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-029.h3: Set up your server for dial-out
guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-029.text: The dial-out server creates outbound calls and manages WebSocket connections. When you trigger a dial-out call, your server:

guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-030.h3: Set up your server for dial-out
guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-030.text:
1. Receives the dial-out request with target phone number and parameters
2. Creates an Exotel call using the Voice API with a callback URL
3. Accepts the WebSocket connection from Exotel
4. Parses call data from the WebSocket messages
5. Runs the Pipecat bot with the call information

guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-049.text: For fully self-hosted production deployment, ensure your servers are:

guides-telephony-exotel-websockets-051.text:

guides-telephony-overview-000.text: You can dial-in to your Pipecat bots, and have them dial-out too, across both PSTN and SIP. The technical implementation will depend on your chosen transport and phone number vendor; each will likely have their own methods and events to consider.

guides-telephony-overview-001.text:
PSTN (Public Switched Telephone Network) : The traditional phone network consisting of physical phone lines, cables, and transmission links. PSTN operates on a one-user-per-line basis.

SIP (Session Initiation Protocol) : A signaling protocol used for voice and video calls over IP networks. SIP can handle multiple users per line and enables advanced call control features like transfers, forwarding, and multi-party calls.

guides-telephony-overview-002.text: Best for: Simple telephony workflows and quick prototypes

guides-telephony-overview-003.text:
- How it works: Real-time audio streaming over WebSocket connections
- Call control: Basic, managed by the telephony provider
- Supported providers: Twilio, Telnyx, Plivo, Exotel
- Limitations: No advanced call center features like transfers or reconnects

guides-telephony-overview-005.text:
- Simple inbound/outbound calling
- Integration with existing Twilio Studio or Flex workflows
- Quick setup with minimal configuration

guides-telephony-overview-006.text: Best for: When connecting web clients to telephony systems or for more complex transfer scenarios

guides-telephony-overview-007.text:
- How it works: Peer-to-peer real-time communication optimized for varying network conditions
- Call control: Advanced, includes track-level control
- Supported providers: Daily (with PSTN integration)
- Benefits: Designed for scale, handles network variations gracefully

guides-telephony-overview-009.text:
- When connecting web or mobile clients to telephony systems
- Applications requiring high-quality audio/video
- Users on devices with varying network conditions

guides-telephony-overview-010.text: Best for: Enterprise telephony and advanced call control scenarios

guides-telephony-overview-011.text:
- How it works: Industry-standard protocol for voice/video calls over IP
- Call control: Full control, including transfers, forwarding, multi-party, etc.
- Integration: Works with legacy call centers and telephony systems
- Vendor flexibility: Supports multiple telephony vendors without platform-specific code

guides-telephony-overview-013.text:
- Multi-agent or multi-party calls
- Integration with legacy call centers
- Advanced features: warm transfers, agent assist, call forwarding
- Enterprise telephony requirements

guides-telephony-overview-014.text:
**Multiple providers:**
You can configure your Pipecat bots to handle multiple
vendors simultaneously. For example, use both Daily and Twilio as phone number
providers concurrently.

guides-telephony-overview-015.text: Direct PSTN connectivity through Daily’s platform with WebRTC transport.

guides-telephony-overview-016.text:
- Setup: Purchase phone numbers directly through Daily
- Transport: WebRTC for optimal audio quality
- Use case: A range of use cases, from simple to complex including cold and warm transfers

guides-telephony-overview-018.text: Combine Daily’s WebRTC transport with SIP-based telephony providers.

guides-telephony-overview-019.text:
- Setup: Use existing Twilio numbers with Daily transport
- Transport: WebRTC with SIP forwarding
- Use case: Leverage existing Twilio workflows with Daily’s transport benefits

guides-telephony-overview-021.text: Direct integration with telephony providers using WebSocket connections.

guides-telephony-overview-022.text: Media Streams integration with Twilio’s telephony platform

guides-telephony-overview-023.text: Real-time media streaming with Telnyx services

guides-telephony-overview-024.text: Voice streaming API integration with Plivo

guides-telephony-overview-025.text: WebSocket integration with Exotel telephony platform

guides-telephony-overview-026.text: Choose the telephony option that best fits your use case and follow the corresponding guide. Each option provides complete setup instructions and example code to get you started quickly.

guides-telephony-plivo-websockets-001.text: Complete dial-in implementation using Plivo Media Streaming over WebSocket

guides-telephony-plivo-websockets-002.text: Outbound calling using Plivo Media Streaming with WebSocket transport

guides-telephony-plivo-websockets-003.text: You’ll need Plivo phone numbers for both dial-in and dial-out functionality:

guides-telephony-plivo-websockets-004.text:
- Visit plivo.com and purchase phone numbers
- Ensure your numbers support Voice capabilities
- Configure XML applications for dial-in numbers (covered below)

guides-telephony-plivo-websockets-005.text: Configure your environment variables for Plivo and AI services:

guides-telephony-plivo-websockets-007.text: Dial-in allows users to call your Plivo number and connect to your Pipecat bot via WebSocket Media Streaming.

guides-telephony-plivo-websockets-008.text: Here’s the sequence of events when someone calls your Plivo number:

guides-telephony-plivo-websockets-009.text:
1. Plivo receives an incoming call to your phone number
2. Plivo calls your XML server with call details
3. Your server responds with XML that establishes a WebSocket connection
4. Plivo opens a WebSocket to your server with real-time audio
5. Your bot processes the audio using the Pipecat pipeline
6. The bot responds with audio sent back to Plivo over WebSocket
7. Plivo plays the audio to the caller in real-time

guides-telephony-plivo-websockets-010.text: Your server handles Plivo webhooks and WebSocket connections for real-time audio streaming. When Plivo receives a call, your server:

guides-telephony-plivo-websockets-011.text:
1. Receives the webhook with call details (CallUUID, caller information)
2. Returns XML that establishes a WebSocket connection to your server
3. Accepts the WebSocket connection from Plivo
4. Parses call data from the WebSocket messages
5. Runs the Pipecat bot with the parsed call information

guides-telephony-plivo-websockets-012.text: The server orchestrates the real-time audio streaming between Plivo’s telephony infrastructure and your Pipecat bot pipeline.

guides-telephony-plivo-websockets-013.text: You can pass custom data to your bot by adding query parameters to the WebSocket URL in your XML response. This works for both dial-in and dial-out scenarios:

guides-telephony-plivo-websockets-014.text:
# Example: Adding custom parameters to the WebSocket URL
xml = f"""<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<Response>
  <Stream bidirectional="true" keepCallAlive="true" contentType="audio/x-mulaw;rate=8000">
    wss://your-server.com/ws?user_id={user_id}&session_type=support&campaign_id=summer_sale
  </Stream>
</Response>"""

guides-telephony-plivo-websockets-015.text: These parameters are accessible in your server code and can be used to customize your bot’s behavior based on the specific call context.

guides-telephony-plivo-websockets-016.text: See the full FastAPI server code with XML generation and WebSocket handling

guides-telephony-plivo-websockets-017.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-in
guides-telephony-plivo-websockets-017.text:
Your bot receives the WebSocket connection and parses the call data to extract call information. The key components are:

WebSocket Parsing : Pipecat’s built-in parser extracts call data from Plivo’s WebSocket messages:

guides-telephony-plivo-websockets-018.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-in
guides-telephony-plivo-websockets-018.text:
from pipecat.runner.utils import parse_telephony_websocket

async def run_bot(websocket: WebSocket):
    # Parse Plivo WebSocket data
    transport_type, call_data = await parse_telephony_websocket(websocket)

    # Extract call information
    stream_id = call_data["stream_id"]
    call_id = call_data["call_id"]  # Plivo's CallUUID

guides-telephony-plivo-websockets-019.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-in
guides-telephony-plivo-websockets-019.text: Transport Creation : Configure the WebSocket transport with Plivo serialization:

guides-telephony-plivo-websockets-020.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-in
guides-telephony-plivo-websockets-020.text:
# Create Plivo serializer with call details
serializer = PlivoFrameSerializer(
    stream_id=stream_id,
    call_id=call_id,
    auth_id=os.getenv("PLIVO_AUTH_ID"),
    auth_token=os.getenv("PLIVO_AUTH_TOKEN"),
)

# Configure WebSocket transport
transport = FastAPIWebsocketTransport(
    websocket=websocket,
    params=FastAPIWebsocketParams(
        audio_in_enabled=True,
        audio_out_enabled=True,
        add_wav_header=False,
        serializer=serializer,
    ),
)

# Your bot pipeline setup here...

guides-telephony-telnyx-websockets-007.text: Dial-in allows users to call your Telnyx number and connect to your Pipecat bot via WebSocket Media Streaming. Unlike Twilio, Telnyx automatically provides caller information (to/from numbers) in the WebSocket messages, so no custom server is needed for basic dial-in functionality.

guides-telephony-telnyx-websockets-008.text: Here’s the sequence of events when someone calls your Telnyx number:

guides-telephony-telnyx-websockets-009.text:
1. Telnyx receives an incoming call to your phone number
2. Telnyx executes your TeXML application which establishes a WebSocket connection
3. Telnyx opens a WebSocket to your server with real-time audio and call metadata
4. Your bot processes the audio using the Pipecat pipeline
5. The bot responds with audio sent back to Telnyx over WebSocket
6. Telnyx plays the audio to the caller in real-time

guides-telephony-telnyx-websockets-010.text: Telnyx uses TeXML (Telnyx Extensible Markup Language) to control call flow. For dial-in, you create a TeXML application that establishes a WebSocket connection directly to your bot:

guides-telephony-telnyx-websockets-012.text: The bidirectionalMode="rtp" parameter enables real-time audio streaming in both directions.

guides-telephony-telnyx-websockets-013.h3: Personalizing Your Bot with Caller Information

guides-telephony-telnyx-websockets-014.text:
- Look up customer information in your database

- Route calls to different bot behaviors (e.g., VIP handling, support vs sales)
- Implement custom business logic based on the caller

guides-telephony-telnyx-websockets-015.h3: Personalizing Your Bot with Caller Information
guides-telephony-telnyx-websockets-015.text: The example bot demonstrates how to extract caller information from Telnyx’s WebSocket messages and use it to personalize the conversation.

guides-telephony-telnyx-websockets-016.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-in
guides-telephony-telnyx-websockets-016.text:
Your bot receives the WebSocket connection and automatically gets call information from Telnyx’s WebSocket messages. The key components are:

WebSocket Parsing : Pipecat’s built-in parser extracts call data from Telnyx’s WebSocket messages:

guides-telephony-telnyx-websockets-017.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-in
guides-telephony-telnyx-websockets-017.text:
from pipecat.runner.utils import parse_telephony_websocket

async def run_bot(websocket: WebSocket):
    # Parse Telnyx WebSocket data
    transport_type, call_data = await parse_telephony_websocket(websocket)

    # Extract call information (automatically provided by Telnyx)
    # Data format: {
    #     "stream_id": str,
    #     "call_control_id": str,
    #     "outbound_encoding": str,
    #     "from": str,
    #     "to": str,
    # }
    stream_id = call_data["stream_id"]
    call_control_id = call_data["call_control_id"]
    outbound_encoding = call_data["outbound_encoding"]
    from_number = call_data["from"]  # Caller's number
    to_number = call_data["to"]      # Your Telnyx number

guides-telephony-telnyx-websockets-018.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-in
guides-telephony-telnyx-websockets-018.text: Transport Creation : Configure the WebSocket transport with Telnyx serialization:

guides-telephony-telnyx-websockets-019.text:
# Create Telnyx serializer with call details
serializer = TelnyxFrameSerializer(
    stream_id=stream_id,
    call_control_id=call_control_id,
    api_key=os.getenv("TELNYX_API_KEY"),
)

# Configure WebSocket transport
transport = FastAPIWebsocketTransport(
    websocket=websocket,
    params=FastAPIWebsocketParams(
        audio_in_enabled=True,
        audio_out_enabled=True,
        add_wav_header=False,
        serializer=serializer,
    ),
)

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-007.text: Dial-in allows users to call your Twilio number and connect to your Pipecat bot via Daily’s WebRTC transport.

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-008.text: Here’s the sequence of events when someone calls your Twilio number:

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-009.text:
1. Twilio receives an incoming call to your phone number
2. Twilio calls your webhook server ( /call endpoint)
3. Your server creates a Daily room with SIP capabilities
4. Your server starts the bot process with the room details
5. Your server responds to Twilio with TwiML that puts the caller on hold with music
6. Upon receiving the on_dialin_ready event , the bot forwards the call to the Daily SIP endpoint
7. The caller and bot are connected , and the bot handles the conversation

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-010.text: Your server acts as the orchestrator between Twilio’s telephony infrastructure and Daily’s WebRTC transport. When Twilio receives a call, it sends a webhook to your server. Your server then:

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-011.text:
1. Extracts call information from Twilio’s webhook (CallSid, caller details)
2. Creates a Daily room with SIP capabilities enabled
3. Spawns a bot process with the room details and call information
4. Responds to Twilio with TwiML that puts the caller on hold with music
5. Coordinates the connection between Twilio SIP and Daily WebRTC

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-012.text: The server handles the complex orchestration between two different telephony systems, ensuring seamless audio quality through Daily’s WebRTC transport while leveraging Twilio’s robust SIP infrastructure.

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-013.text:
See the full FastAPI server code with Twilio webhook handling and Daily room
creation

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-014.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-in
guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-014.text:
The bot receives arguments from the server process and uses them to coordinate between Daily’s WebRTC transport and Twilio’s SIP infrastructure. The key components are:

Bot Entry Point : The bot receives arguments containing:

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-015.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-in
guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-015.text:
- room_url : Daily room URL for the WebRTC connection
- token : Daily room token for authentication
- call_sid : Twilio call identifier for SIP forwarding
- sip_endpoint : Daily’s SIP endpoint URL for call routing

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-016.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-in
guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-016.text: Transport Creation : These arguments are used to configure the DailyTransport for WebRTC:

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-017.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-in
guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-017.text:
from pipecat.transports.daily import DailyTransport, DailyParams

async def run_bot(room_url: str, token: str, call_sid: str, sip_endpoint: str):
    # Create Daily WebRTC transport
    transport = DailyTransport(
        room_url,
        token,
        "Voice Bot",
        DailyParams(
            audio_in_enabled=True,
            audio_out_enabled=True,
            video_out_enabled=False,
            transcription_enabled=True,
        )
    )

    # Handle when Daily SIP endpoint is ready for call forwarding
    @transport.event_handler("on_dialin_ready")
    async def on_dialin_ready(transport, cdata):
        # Forward the Twilio call to Daily's SIP endpoint
        twilio_client.calls(call_sid).update(
            twiml=f"<Response><Dial><Sip>{sip_endpoint}</Sip></Dial></Response>"
        )
        logger.info("Call forwarded from Twilio to Daily SIP endpoint")

    # Your bot pipeline setup here...

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-018.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-in
guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-018.text: Call Forwarding Flow : The on_dialin_ready event is crucial. It signals when Daily’s SIP infrastructure is ready to receive the call. At this point, the bot uses Twilio’s API to update the call with new TwiML that forwards the audio from Twilio’s SIP to Daily’s SIP endpoint, completing the connection.

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-026.text: Here’s the sequence of events for dial-out calls:

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-027.text:
1. Your application triggers a dial-out (via API call or user action)
2. Server creates a Daily room with SIP capabilities
3. Bot joins the Daily room and sets up the WebRTC transport
4. Bot initiates SIP call through Twilio to the target number
5. Twilio routes the call to the destination phone number
6. The recipient answers and is connected to your bot via Daily WebRTC
7. The bot handles the conversation with high-quality WebRTC audio

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-028.h3: Set up your server for dial-out
guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-028.text: The dial-out server orchestrates outbound calls by coordinating between your application, Daily’s WebRTC transport, and Twilio’s SIP infrastructure. When you trigger a dial-out call, your server:

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-029.h3: Set up your server for dial-out
guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-029.text:
1. Receives the dial-out request with target phone number and call parameters
2. Creates a Daily room with SIP capabilities and dial-out enabled
3. Spawns a bot process with the room details and target phone number
4. Initiates the SIP call through Twilio to the destination number
5. Manages the connection between Daily WebRTC and Twilio SIP routing
6. Handles call events like answered, busy, or failed connections

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-030.h3: Set up your server for dial-out
guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-030.text: The server simplifies outbound calling by abstracting the complexity of coordinating between WebRTC transport and SIP telephony, providing high-quality audio through Daily while leveraging Twilio’s reliable call routing.

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-031.text:
See the full FastAPI server code with Daily room creation and SIP dial-out
management

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-032.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-out
guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-032.text:
The dial-out bot receives arguments from the server process and uses them to initiate outbound calls through Twilio’s SIP infrastructure via Daily’s WebRTC transport. The key components are:

Bot Entry Point : The bot receives arguments containing:

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-033.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-out
guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-033.text:
- room_url : Daily room URL for the WebRTC connection
- token : Daily room token for authentication
- target_number : Phone number to call via Twilio SIP
- sip_uri : Twilio SIP URI for routing the outbound call

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-034.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-out
guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-034.text: Transport Creation : These arguments are used to configure the DailyTransport for dial-out:

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-035.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-out
guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-035.text:
from pipecat.transports.daily import DailyTransport, DailyParams

async def run_bot(room_url: str, token: str, target_number: str, sip_uri: str):
    # Create Daily WebRTC transport
    transport = DailyTransport(
        room_url,
        token,
        "Voice Bot",
        DailyParams(
            audio_in_enabled=True,
            audio_out_enabled=True,
            video_out_enabled=False,
            transcription_enabled=True,
        )
    )

    # Initiate the outbound call through Twilio SIP
    await transport.start_dialout(sip_uri)

    # Your bot pipeline setup here...

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-036.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-out
guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-036.text: Dial-out Flow : The start_dialout() method initiates the call by connecting Daily’s WebRTC transport to Twilio’s SIP infrastructure. The sip_uri parameter contains the Twilio SIP endpoint configured with the target phone number, allowing Daily to route the call through Twilio’s telephony network to reach the destination.

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-037.text: See the full bot.py with WebRTC transport setup and SIP dial-out configuration

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-039.text:
1. Start your server : Run your FastAPI server
2. Trigger a call : Make an API request to start a dial-out call
3. Answer your phone : The bot will call the specified number via Twilio
4. Talk to your bot : Have a conversation with high-quality WebRTC audio

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-040.text: See the full README with step-by-step setup, API usage, and SIP configuration

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-041.text: Always respond to Twilio’s initial webhook with hold music to give your bot time to initialize:

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-043.text: Avoid using <Pause> - Twilio’s pause duration is limited and may not provide enough time for Daily SIP setup.

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-044.text: Use a flag to ensure you only forward calls once when handling multiple on_dialin_ready events:

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-045.text:
call_already_forwarded = False

@transport.event_handler("on_dialin_ready")
async def on_dialin_ready(transport, cdata):
    nonlocal call_already_forwarded
    if call_already_forwarded:
        return
    # Forward the call...
    call_already_forwarded = True

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-046.text: Use ngrok to expose your local server for testing:

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-047.text:
python server.py
ngrok http 8000
# Use the ngrok URL as your Twilio webhook

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-048.text: For production deployment on Pipecat Cloud, your webhook server calls the /{agent}/start endpoint instead of spawning local bot processes:

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-050.text: The room information and call parameters are passed in the body field, which your Pipecat Cloud bot receives as arguments.

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-051.text: For self-hosted production deployment, ensure your servers are:

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-052.text:
- Publicly accessible with HTTPS
- Able to handle concurrent webhook requests
- Properly configured with both Twilio and Daily API credentials

guides-telephony-twilio-daily-sip-053.text:
- Explore the complete examples for full implementations
- Learn about Daily PSTN integration for direct phone number provisioning
- Check out Twilio WebSocket integration for simpler telephony workflows

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-000.text:
- An active Twilio account with API credentials
- One or more Twilio provisioned phone numbers
- A tunneling service like ngrok for local development
- API keys for speech-to-text, text-to-speech, and LLM services

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-001.text: Complete dial-in implementation using Twilio Media Streams over WebSocket

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-002.text: Outbound calling using Twilio Media Streams with WebSocket transport

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-003.text: You’ll need Twilio phone numbers for both dial-in and dial-out functionality:

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-004.text:
- Visit console.twilio.com and purchase phone numbers
- Ensure your numbers support Voice capabilities
- Configure webhook URLs for dial-in numbers (covered below)

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-005.text: Configure your environment variables for Twilio and AI services:

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-007.text: Dial-in allows users to call your Twilio number and connect to your Pipecat bot via WebSocket Media Streams.

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-008.text: Here’s the sequence of events when someone calls your Twilio number:

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-009.text:
1. Twilio sends WebSocket messages : Twilio processes the associated TwiML Bin and starts a WebSocket stream to your bot (local or Pipecat Cloud)
2. Parse the WebSocket messages : Your bot parses the WebSocket connection messages to set up the corresponding Pipecat transport
3. (Optional) Look up the caller : Optionally, look up the caller using Twilio’s REST API to retrieve custom information about the call and personalize your bot’s behavior
4. Bot starts responding : Once the pipeline is started, your bot will initiate the conversation

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-010.text:
**​1. Create a TwiML Bin**

A TwiML Bin tells Twilio how to handle incoming calls. You’ll create one that establishes a WebSocket connection to your bot.

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-011.text:
1. Go to the Twilio Console
2. Navigate to TwiML Bins → My TwiML Bins
3. Click the + to create a new TwiML Bin
4. Name your bin and add the TwiML:

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-014.text: **​2. Assign TwiML Bin to Your Phone Number**

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-015.text:
1. Navigate to Phone Numbers → Manage → Active Numbers
2. Click on your Twilio phone number
3. In the “Voice Configuration” section:
  - Set “A call comes in” to TwiML Bin
  - Select the TwiML Bin you created
4. Click Save configuration

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-016.text: You’ll need two terminal windows to run the example.

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-017.text:
1. In your first terminal, start ngrok to expose your local server: Copy ngrok http 7860 Tip: Use the --subdomain flag for a reusable ngrok URL.
2. In your second terminal, install dependencies: Copy uv sync
3. Then run the bot: Copy uv run bot.py -t twilio
4. Call your bot by placing a call to the number associated with your bot. The bot will answer and start the conversation.

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-018.h3: Personalizing Your Bot with Caller Information
guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-018.text: When a call comes in, your bot receives a Call SID from Twilio. You can use this Call SID to fetch caller information from Twilio’s REST API, including the caller’s phone number. With this information, you can:

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-019.h3: Personalizing Your Bot with Caller Information
guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-019.text:
- Look up customer information in your database
- Personalize greetings and responses based on caller identity
- Route calls to different bot behaviors (e.g., VIP handling, support vs sales)
- Implement custom business logic based on the caller

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-020.h3: Personalizing Your Bot with Caller Information
guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-020.text: The example bot demonstrates how to fetch caller information asynchronously using aiohttp to avoid blocking the event loop, ensuring your bot remains responsive even with multiple concurrent calls.

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-021.text:
See the full implementation with WebSocket parsing, REST API integration, and
caller personalization

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-022.text: Dial-out allows your bot to initiate calls to phone numbers using Twilio’s outbound calling capabilities with WebSocket Media Streams.

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-023.text: Here’s the sequence of events for dial-out calls:

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-024.text:
1. Your application triggers a dial-out (via API call or user action)
2. Server initiates a Twilio call to the target phone number
3. Twilio establishes the call and opens a WebSocket connection
4. Your bot joins the WebSocket and sets up the pipeline
5. The recipient answers and is connected to your bot
6. The bot handles the conversation with real-time audio streaming

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-025.h3: Set up your server for dial-out
guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-025.text: The dial-out server creates outbound calls and manages WebSocket connections. When you trigger a dial-out call, your server:

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-026.h3: Set up your server for dial-out
guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-026.text:
1. Receives the dial-out request with target phone number and parameters
2. Creates a Twilio call using the REST API with TwiML that establishes WebSocket
3. Accepts the WebSocket connection from Twilio
4. Parses call data including any custom parameters
5. Runs the Pipecat bot with the call information

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-027.text:
See the full FastAPI server code with outbound call creation and WebSocket
handling

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-028.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-out
guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-028.text:
The dial-out bot configuration is similar to dial-in, but you can pass custom parameters when creating the outbound call:

Custom Parameters for Dial-out : Pass information to your bot through TwiML parameters:

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-029.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-out
guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-029.text:
# When creating the outbound call, include custom parameters
twiml = f"""
<Response>
  <Connect>
    <Stream url="wss://your-server.com/ws">
      <Parameter name="campaign_id" value="summer_sale" />
      <Parameter name="customer_id" value="{customer_id}" />
      <Parameter name="call_type" value="outbound" />
    </Stream>
  </Connect>
</Response>
"""

# Create the outbound call
call = twilio_client.calls.create(
    to=target_number,
    from_=your_twilio_number,
    twiml=twiml
)

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-030.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-out
guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-030.text: Bot Configuration : The bot receives and uses these parameters:

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-031.h3: Configure your Pipecat bot for dial-out
guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-031.text:
# Parse WebSocket data (same as dial-in)
transport_type, call_data = await parse_telephony_websocket(websocket)

# Access custom parameters
custom_params = call_data["body"]
campaign_id = custom_params.get("campaign_id")
customer_id = custom_params.get("customer_id")

# Customize bot behavior for outbound calls
if campaign_id == "summer_sale":
    greeting = f"Hi! I'm calling about our summer sale promotion..."

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-032.text: See the full bot.py with outbound call handling and parameter usage

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-034.text:
1. Start your server : Run your FastAPI server
2. Trigger a call : Make an API request to start an outbound call
3. Answer your phone : The bot will call the specified number
4. Talk to your bot : Have a conversation with your AI agent

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-035.text:
See the full README with step-by-step setup, API usage, and outbound call
configuration

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-036.text: Twilio Media Streams uses 8kHz mono audio with 16-bit PCM encoding. To avoid resampling, set the audio input and output sample rate to 8000 Hz:

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-037.text:
task = PipelineTask(
    pipeline,
    params=PipelineParams(
        audio_in_sample_rate=8000,
        audio_out_sample_rate=8000,
    ),
)

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-038.text: When you provide Twilio credentials to the TwilioFrameSerializer , it automatically ends calls when your pipeline ends:

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-040.text: For production deployment without managing your own infrastructure, use Pipecat Cloud:

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-041.text: Deploy Twilio WebSocket bots with automatic scaling and managed infrastructure

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-042.text: For fully self-hosted production deployment, ensure your servers are:

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-043.text:
- Publicly accessible with HTTPS
- Able to handle concurrent WebSocket connections
- Properly configured with Twilio API credentials
- Implementing proper error handling and logging

guides-telephony-twilio-websockets-044.text:
- Explore the complete examples for full implementations
- Learn about Daily + Twilio SIP integration for advanced telephony scenarios
- Check out Daily PSTN integration for direct phone number provisioning
